passages.title: Cap. I · Salūtātiōnēs
passages.latin:
Salvē, discipula.
Salvē, magistra.
Salvē, discipule.
Salvē, magister.
Salvēte, discipulī.
Valē, discipula.
Valē, magistra.
Valēte, discipulī.
passages.english: Hello, student (f.). Hello, teacher (f.). Hello, student (m.). Hello, teacher (m.). Hello, students. Goodbye, student (f.). Goodbye, teacher (f.). Goodbye, students.
passages.notes: Pure greetings. Salvē is singular, Salvēte plural; same for Valē/Valēte. The -a ending marks feminine, -e/-us masculine.
vocab_hints: salvē / salvēte = hello (to one / to many)
valē / valēte = goodbye (to one / to many)

passages.title: Cap. I · Hic et Haec
passages.latin:
Salvēte, discipulī.
Salvē, magister.
Hic est discipulus. Haec est discipula.
Hic est puer. Haec est puella.
Hae sunt puellae. Hī sunt puerī.
Haec est magistra.
"Salvēte, discipulī, puerī et puellae."
Puer est discipulus.
Puella est discipula.
Hae sunt discipulae.
Hī sunt discipulī.
Haec est puella.
passages.english: Hello, students. Hello, teacher (m.). This is a male student. This is a female student. This is a boy. This is a girl. These are girls. These are boys. This is a teacher. "Hello, students, boys and girls." A boy is a student. A girl is a student. These are female students. These are male students. This is a girl.
passages.notes: Hic points to a masculine noun, haec to feminine. Hī/hae are their plurals. Est = is, sunt = are.

passages.title: Cap. I · Adiectīva
passages.latin:
Magistra nōn est puella.
Magistra est fēmina.
Haec est magistra. Haec est fēmina. Haec est puella. Haec puella est discipula. Haec puella nōn est magistra.
Haec puella est parva. Haec puella nōn est parva; haec puella est magna.
Haec puella nōn est parva; haec puella est alta.
Haec puella est bona. Fēmina quoque est bona.
Haec puella nōn est bona.
Haec puella est magna. Magistra quoque est magna.
Haec puella est parva.
Puella est pulchra quoque.
passages.english: A teacher is not a girl. A teacher is a woman. This is a teacher. This is a woman. This is a girl. This girl is a student. This girl is not a teacher. This girl is small. This girl is not small; this girl is large. This girl is not small; this girl is tall. This girl is good. The woman is also good. This girl is not good. This girl is large. The teacher is also large. This girl is small. The girl is also beautiful.
passages.notes: Nōn negates. Quoque means 'also.' All adjectives here end in -a to agree with feminine nouns.

passages.title: Cap. II · Māter et Fīlia
passages.latin:
Hīc sunt fēmina et puer et puella. (Sunt hīc; hīc nōn sunt.)

Haec fēmina est māter; hic puer est fīlius et haec puella est fīlia fēminae. Māter est magna, sed fīlius et fīlia sunt parvī. Haec māter est bona māter. Haec māter nōn est mea māter; nōn est tua māter; est māter puerī et puellae. Haec puella est soror puerī. Hic puer est frāter puellae. Hic puer puellam amat. Haec māter fīliam amat. Haec puella magistram amat et magistra puellam amat.

Fīlium et fīliam fēminae amō. Mātrem puerī et puellae amō. Mātrem meam quoque amō. Sorōrem et frātrem et mātrem amō. Magistram amō. Estne fēmina, puella, māter tua?

Ita. Māter mea est.

Haec puella nōn est soror mea. Soror puerī est. Hic frāter est frāter puellae; nōn est frāter meus. Haec fēmina nōn est māter mea, sed fēminam amō. Estne haec magistra māter tua?

Māter mea nōn est.

Fīlius fēminae est discipulus bonus, et magistra puerum amat. Nunc discipula nōn est bona. Nunc magistra discipulam nōn amat.
passages.english:
Here are a woman, a boy, and a girl. (They are here; they are not here.)

This woman is a mother; this boy is her son and this girl is the woman's daughter. The mother is large, but the son and daughter are small. This mother is a good mother. This mother is not my mother; she is not your mother; she is the mother of the boy and girl. This girl is the boy's sister. This boy is the girl's brother. This boy loves the girl. This mother loves her daughter. This girl loves the teacher and the teacher loves the girl.

I love the woman's son and daughter. I love the mother of the boy and girl. I love my own mother too. I love my sister and brother and mother. I love the teacher. Is this woman the girl's mother?

Yes. She is my mother.

This girl is not my sister. She is the boy's sister. This brother is the girl's brother; he is not my brother. This woman is not my mother, but I love the woman. Is this teacher your mother?

She is not my mother.

The woman's son is a good student, and the teacher loves the boy. Now the student is not good. Now the teacher does not love the student.
passages.notes: First accusative forms: puellam, māter, magistram. Nouns ending in -am are direct objects. Sed = but; nunc = now.

passages.title: Cap. II · Colloquium
passages.latin:
Magistra: Puellam parvam amō.
Puella: Et magistram amō.
Magistra: Māter quoque puellam amat. Amāsne puellam?
Discipula: Ita. Puellam amō.

Valēte, discipulī.

Valē, magistra. Salvē, puella. Haec est magistra; nōn est magister. Estne magistra tua bona?

Ita. Bona est.

Estne frāter tuus discipulus bonus?

Frāter meus est bonus et magnus et altus, sed nōn est pulcher. Soror mea et frāter meus sunt discipulī magistrae bonae.

Valēte, discipulī.
passages.english:
Teacher: I love the little girl. Girl: And I love the teacher. Teacher: The mother also loves the girl. Do you love the girl? Student: Yes. I love the girl.

Goodbye, students.

Goodbye, teacher. Hello, girl. This is a (female) teacher; she is not a (male) teacher. Is your teacher good?

Yes. She is good.

Is your brother a good student?

My brother is good and large and tall, but he is not handsome. My sister and my brother are students of a good teacher.

Goodbye, students.
passages.notes: Amāsne? = Do you love? The -ne ending turns a statement into a yes/no question. Tuus/tua = your (m./f.).

passages.title: Cap. III · Patria Mea
passages.latin:
Fēmina: Salvēte, puer et puella. Hic est fīlius meus; haec est fīlia mea.
Puer: Haec est māter mea; haec est soror mea.
Puella: Haec est māter mea et hic est frāter meus.
Fēmina: Amāsne magistram, puer?
Puer: Ita. Magistram meam amō.
Fēmina: Estne magistra tua hīc?
Puer: Nunc hīc nōn est.
Fēmina: Amāsne magistram tuam, puella?
Puella: Ita. Magistra est bona et magistram bonam amō.

America est patria mea. Americam amō. America est terra magna. America nōn est īnsula. Britannia est īnsula, Hibernia est īnsula, Sicilia est īnsula, Cuba est īnsula. Italia quoque nōn est īnsula. Italia nōn est patria mea sed Italiam amō. Amāsne Italiam? Amāsne Britanniam?
passages.english:
Woman: Hello, boy and girl. This is my son; this is my daughter. Boy: This is my mother; this is my sister. Girl: This is my mother and this is my brother. Woman: Do you love your teacher, boy? Boy: Yes. I love my teacher. Woman: Is your teacher here? Boy: She is not here now. Woman: Do you love your teacher, girl? Girl: Yes. The teacher is good and I love my good teacher.

America is my homeland. I love America. America is a great land. America is not an island. Britain is an island, Ireland is an island, Sicily is an island, Cuba is an island. Italy is also not an island. Italy is not my homeland but I love Italy. Do you love Italy? Do you love Britain?
passages.notes: Patria = homeland/fatherland. Terra = land. Īnsula = island. Notice the accusative -am endings on country names when they are the object of amō.

passages.title: Cap. III · Via Longa
passages.latin:
America nōn est īnsula; est terra magna. Italia et Hispānia nōn sunt terrae magnae sed nōn sunt īnsulae. America est patria mea, sed Italia nōn est patria mea et Britannia nōn est patria mea. America est patria mea et fāma Americae est magna et fortūna Americae est bona. Americam et fāmam Americae et fortūnam Americae amō.

Haec via est longa. Haec via est via bona et pulchra. Nova quoque est haec via. Haec via nōn est via antīqua. Hīc viae nōn sunt antīquae. Viae sunt novae. Viam longam amō. Amatne soror tua viam longam? Amatne frāter viam longam? Amāsne viam longam?
passages.english:
America is not an island; it is a great land. Italy and Spain are not great lands but they are not islands. America is my homeland, but Italy is not my homeland and Britain is not my homeland. America is my homeland and the fame of America is great and the fortune of America is good. I love America and the fame of America and the fortune of America.

This road is long. This road is a good and beautiful road. This road is also new. This road is not an old road. These roads here are not ancient. The roads are new. I love a long road. Does your sister love a long road? Does your brother love a long road? Do you love a long road?
passages.notes: Fāma Americae = fame of America — first genitive (possession). Antīqua/nova = ancient/new. Via = road/way.

passages.title: Cap. IV · Quis et Ubi
passages.latin:
Haec via est via antīqua; nōn est via nova. Italia est terra magna; nōn est īnsula. Sunt longae viae in Italiā. Hae viae sunt antīquae quoque. Sed nunc viae bonae sunt. Fāma Italiae est magna. Fortūna Italiae nōn est bona. Italia nōn est patria mea. America est patria mea. Estne America patria tua?

Ita. America est patria mea.

Quis est haec? Puella est haec; magistra haec nōn est.
Quis est haec puella? Iūlia est haec puella.
Ubi est Iūlia? In viā est Iūlia.
Quid habet Iūlia? Epistulam habet Iūlia.
Cūr habet Iūlia epistulam? Iūlia epistulam habet quod māter Iūliae hīc nōn est. Māter est in Britanniā. Nunc Iūlia epistulam portat et laeta est.
passages.english:
This road is an ancient road; it is not a new road. Italy is a great land; it is not an island. There are long roads in Italy. These roads are also ancient. But now the roads are good. The fame of Italy is great. The fortune of Italy is not good. Italy is not my homeland. America is my homeland. Is America your homeland?

Yes. America is my homeland.

Who is this? This is a girl; this is not a teacher. Who is this girl? Julia is this girl. Where is Julia? Julia is on the road. What does Julia have? Julia has a letter. Why does Julia have a letter? Julia has the letter because Julia's mother is not here. The mother is in Britain. Now Julia carries the letter and she is happy.
passages.notes: Question words: Quis? = Who?, Ubi? = Where?, Quid? = What?, Cūr? = Why?. Quod = because. In + ablative = location.

passages.title: Cap. IV · Agricola
passages.latin:
Nunc Iūlia in casā est. Epistula est longa.

Cūr Iūlia nōn est in scholā? Nōn intellegō.

Puer: Salvē, soror.
Puella: Salvē, frāter.
Puer: Estne māter hīc?
Puella: Nunc hīc māter nōn est. Quid cupis?
Puer: Mātrem cupiō. Valē.
Puella: Valē.

Quis est hic? Agricola est hic. Agricola fīliam pulchram habet et fīliam amat. Fīlium quoque habet. Fīlius agricolae est magnus et labōrat. Sed fīlia nōn est magna; est parva. Fīlia in casā labōrat; fīlius in agrō labōrat. Agricola quoque in agrō labōrat. Fīlia in agrō nōn labōrat et haec est causa: quod est puella et puella parva. Fortūna agricolae nōn est magna sed fīlia parva in agrō nōn labōrat. In casā labōrat et cēnam parat. Agricola est laetus quod fīlia cēnam parat. Cēnam cupit.
passages.english:
Now Julia is in the house. The letter is long.

Why is Julia not in school? I do not understand.

Boy: Hello, sister. Girl: Hello, brother. Boy: Is mother here? Girl: Mother is not here now. What do you want? Boy: I want mother. Goodbye. Girl: Goodbye.

Who is this? This is a farmer. The farmer has a beautiful daughter and loves his daughter. He also has a son. The farmer's son is large and works. But the daughter is not large; she is small. The daughter works in the house; the son works in the field. The farmer also works in the field. The daughter does not work in the field and this is the reason: because she is a girl and a small girl. The farmer's fortune is not great but the little daughter does not work in the field. She works in the house and prepares dinner. The farmer is happy because the daughter prepares dinner. He wants dinner.
passages.notes: Causa = reason/cause. Labōrat = works. Cēnam parat = prepares dinner. Casa = house. Ager/agrō = field (note the ablative form in agrō).

passages.title: Cap. V · Nauta
passages.latin:
Quis est hic vir? Hic vir est nauta. Nauta est amīcus agricolae. Vir altus et magnus est. Nauta agricolam amat et agricola nautam amat. Nauta labōrat sed nōn in agrō. In terrā nōn labōrat. Estne nauta Americānus? Ita. Hic vir saepe in terrā est. Nōn semper in terrā est. Interdum in casā agricolae est. Intellegisne? Ita.

Agricola hunc amīcum amat et saepe nautam laudat quod nauta bonus est. Fīlius et fīlia agricolae quoque nautam vidēre cupiunt, quod vīta nautae est perīculōsa. Hic nauta multās terrās videt quod nōn est semper in Americā. Aquam saepe videt quod est nauta. Aqua saepe est perīculōsa. Interdum nauta aquam vidēre nōn cupit.
passages.english:
Who is this man? This man is a sailor. The sailor is a friend of the farmer. The man is tall and large. The sailor loves the farmer and the farmer loves the sailor. The sailor works but not in a field. He does not work on land. Is the sailor American? Yes. This man is often on land. He is not always on land. Sometimes he is in the farmer's house. Do you understand? Yes.

The farmer loves this friend and often praises the sailor because the sailor is good. The farmer's son and daughter also want to see the sailor, because the sailor's life is dangerous. This sailor sees many lands because he is not always in America. He often sees water because he is a sailor. Water is often dangerous. Sometimes the sailor does not want to see water.
passages.notes: Saepe = often; semper = always; interdum = sometimes. Vīta = life. Perīculōsa = dangerous. Laudat = praises.

passages.title: Cap. VI · Magistra Nārrat
passages.latin:
Ego sum magistra. Tū es discipulus. Ego in Americā habitō. Habitāsne tū in Americā? Haec est pictūra scholae meae. In pictūrā est puer, discipulus bonus. Pater huius puerī est nauta. Incola Americae est. Vir bonus et magnus est. Nauta epistulās scrībit et puer saepe mihi epistulās ostendit. Interdum nauta est in scholā. Discipulīs fābulās dē multīs terrīs nārrat et pictūrās ostendit. Nauta multās terrās videt et virōs et fēminās videt. Discipulī pictūrās nautae spectant et fābulās laudant. Interdum ego epistulās nautae legō. Discipulī nautae grātiās agunt quod pictūrās ostendit et fābulās nārrat et epistulās bene scrībit.

Fīlius nautae est saepe laetus quod pater dōna dat. Saepe fīliō dōna dat. Puer patrī grātiās agit.
passages.english:
I am a teacher. You are a student. I live in America. Do you live in America? This is a picture of my school. In the picture is a boy, a good student. This boy's father is a sailor. He is an inhabitant of America. He is a good and large man. The sailor writes letters and the boy often shows me the letters. Sometimes the sailor is in the school. He tells the students stories about many lands and shows pictures. The sailor sees many lands and sees men and women. The students look at the sailor's pictures and praise the stories. Sometimes I read the sailor's letters. The students thank the sailor because he shows pictures and tells stories and writes letters well.

The sailor's son is often happy because the father gives gifts. He often gives gifts to his son. The boy thanks his father.
passages.notes: Ego = I; tū = you. Habitō = I live/dwell. Grātiās agit = gives thanks. Dōnum = gift. Nārrat = tells (a story). Ostendit = shows.

passages.title: Cap. VII · Poēta et Casa
passages.latin:
Epistulam habeō. Epistulam nautae ostendō. Nauta epistulam videt. Epistulam spectat. Est epistula nautae. Nauta epistulam cupit. Nautae epistulam dō. Nunc epistulam habet et laetus est. Mihi grātiās agit. Epistulam legit. In epistulā est pictūra. Nunc mihi pictūram mōnstrat.

Haec est casa Americāna. Haec casa iānuam habet. Iānua est clausa. Multās fenestrās quoque habet. Fenestrae sunt magnae.

Haec est casa Rōmāna. Casa nōn est magna; est parva. Iānuam habet. Iānua est aperta. Fenestrās habet sed nōn multās fenestrās. Fenestrae Rōmānae nōn sunt magnae; sunt parvae. Fenestrae Rōmānae sunt altae. Nōn sunt clārae. Fenestrās Rōmānās nōn amō. Fenestrās Americānās laudō.

Hīc non habitat vir. Ibi habitat vir. Hic vir est poēta clārus et benignus. Casam habet et cūrat. Nunc fēminae in silvā ambulant. Nunc poēta iānuam aperit et fēminās videt. Fēminae rosās portant. Poētae rosās ostendunt et poēta est laetus quod rosae sunt grātae et rosās nōn habet. Fēminae poētae rosās dant. Poēta fēminīs grātiās agit.
passages.english:
I have a letter. I show the letter to the sailor. The sailor sees the letter. He looks at it. It is the sailor's letter. The sailor wants the letter. I give the letter to the sailor. Now he has the letter and he is happy. He thanks me. He reads the letter. In the letter is a picture. Now he shows me the picture.

This is an American house. This house has a door. The door is closed. It also has many windows. The windows are large.

This is a Roman house. The house is not large; it is small. It has a door. The door is open. It has windows but not many windows. The Roman windows are not large; they are small. The Roman windows are tall. They are not bright. I do not love the Roman windows. I praise the American windows.

A man does not live here. A man lives there. This man is a famous and kind poet. He has a house and cares for it. Now women are walking in the forest. Now the poet opens the door and sees the women. The women are carrying roses. They show the roses to the poet and the poet is happy because the roses are pleasing and he does not have roses. The women give roses to the poet. The poet thanks the women.
passages.notes: Iānua = door; fenestra = window; silva = forest/woods. Apertus/clausus = open/closed. Benignus = kind. Grātus = pleasing/welcome.

passages.title: Cap. VIII · Schola Nostra
passages.latin:
In viā ambulō. Hīc est casa pulchra. Fenestra casae est aperta et in fenestrā est rosa pulchra. Iānua casae nōn est clausa. Iānua quoque est aperta et hīc est fēmina benigna.

Haec est schola nostra. Schola nostra est clāra et fāma est magna. Interdum iānua est aperta. Rosās vidēmus. Agrōs quoque vidēmus. Silvam ex fenestrīs vidēmus. Ibi est via longa. Ex fenestrīs viam quoque vidēmus.

Nunc magistra nostra discipulōs exspectat. In scholā nōn labōrāmus. In viīs ambulāmus. Ad scholam properāmus. Hī puerī linguam Latīnam discunt, sed hae puellae sunt parvae et linguam Latīnam non discunt. Magistra nostra est benigna et puerīs et puellīs cāra. Linguam Latīnam discere cupimus. Interdum mātrēs nostrae ad scholam properant. Mātrēs nostrae magistram vident et laudant. Nōs quoque magistram laudāmus.
passages.english:
I am walking on the road. Here is a beautiful house. The window of the house is open and in the window is a beautiful rose. The door of the house is not closed. The door is also open and here is a kind woman.

This is our school. Our school is famous and its reputation is great. Sometimes the door is open. We see roses. We also see fields. We see the forest from the windows. There is a long road over there. We also see the road from the windows.

Now our teacher awaits the students. We are not working in school. We are walking on the roads. We are hurrying to school. These boys are learning the Latin language, but these girls are small and do not learn the Latin language. Our teacher is kind and dear to boys and girls. We want to learn the Latin language. Sometimes our mothers hurry to school. Our mothers see and praise the teacher. We also praise the teacher.
passages.notes: Nōs = we. Nostra = our. Vidēmus/ambulāmus = we see/we walk (1st plural). Properāmus = we hurry. Discunt = they learn. Linguam Latīnam = Latin language (accusative).

passages.title: Cap. IX · Medicus Noctū
passages.latin:
Nox est et noctū agricolae agrōs nōn cūrant. Noctū mātrēs cēnam nōn parant. Noctū discipulī in scholā nōn labōrant et linguam Latīnam nōn discunt. Sed noctū nautae saepe labōrant.

Nox est et ibi medicus ambulat. Medicus noster nōn est, sed medicus puerī. Nunc medicus properat sed est tardus. In silvā properat. Puer, fīlius agricolae, in casā est aeger. Medicus, vir clārus et benignus, ad casam agricolae properat quod puer est aeger. Nox est et medicus sōlus ambulat. Amīcī cum medicō nōn ambulant. Sōlus properat. Tardus est quod lūna est obscūra. Medicus lūnam clāram vidēre cupit. Stellae quoque sunt obscūrae et medicus nōn clārē videt.

Puer laetus nōn est. Aeger et miser est. Ubi nōs aegrī sumus, miserī sumus nōs quoque. Puer sōlus est, sed sōlus esse nōn cupit. Amīcōs vidēre cupit. Lūnam et stellās nōn videt quod obscūrae sunt. Puer aeger medicum exspectat sed medicus est tardus.
passages.english:
It is night and at night farmers do not tend their fields. At night mothers do not prepare dinner. At night students do not work in school and do not learn the Latin language. But at night sailors often work.

It is night and there a doctor is walking. He is not our doctor, but the boy's doctor. Now the doctor hurries but he is slow. He hurries in the forest. The boy, the farmer's son, is sick in the house. The doctor, a famous and kind man, hurries to the farmer's house because the boy is sick. It is night and the doctor walks alone. Friends do not walk with the doctor. He hurries alone. He is slow because the moon is dark. The doctor wants to see a bright moon. The stars are also dark and the doctor does not see clearly.

The boy is not happy. He is sick and miserable. When we are sick, we are also miserable. The boy is alone, but he does not want to be alone. He wants to see friends. He does not see the moon and stars because they are dark. The sick boy awaits the doctor but the doctor is slow.
passages.notes: Nox/noctū = night/at night. Aeger = sick. Miser = miserable. Sōlus = alone. Lūna = moon. Stella = star. Tardus = slow/late. Obscūrus = dark/dim.

passages.title: Cap. IX · Aegrī
passages.latin:
Nunc medicus iānuam agricolae aperit et puerum spectat. Medicus est puerō benignus. Puerō fābulās longās et bonās nārrat. Ex fenestrīs puer et medicus silvam spectant sed stellās et lūnam nōn vident. Nunc medicus iānuam claudit. Ex casā agricolae properat et ad silvam ambulat.

Estne aegra, puella?
Minimē. Nōn sum aegra.
Laeta sum quod nōn es aegra. Misera sum quod Cornēlia est aegra. Haec puella nōn est aegra; haec puella nōn est aegra. Nōn sunt aegrae. Estisne aegrae, puellae?
Nōn sumus aegrae.
Estne aeger, puer?
Nōn sum aeger.
Laeta sum quod nōn es aeger. Esne aeger, discipule?
Nōn sum aeger.
Estisne, puerī, aegrī?
Minimē. Nōn sumus aegrī.
Laeta sum.
passages.english:
Now the doctor opens the farmer's door and looks at the boy. The doctor is kind to the boy. He tells the boy long and good stories. From the windows the boy and the doctor look at the forest but do not see the stars and moon. Now the doctor closes the door. He hurries out of the farmer's house and walks toward the forest.

Are you sick, girl? No indeed. I am not sick. I am happy because you are not sick. I am sad because Cornelia is sick. This girl is not sick; this girl is not sick. They are not sick. Are you sick, girls? We are not sick. Are you sick, boy? I am not sick. I am happy because you are not sick. Are you sick, student? I am not sick. Are you sick, boys? No indeed. We are not sick. I am happy.
passages.notes: Minimē = No indeed / Not at all. Estisne? = Are you (plural)? Sumus = we are. The adjective aeger/aegra changes ending to match gender.
vocab_hints: minimē = no indeed, not at all

passages.title: Cap. X · Vesper
passages.latin:
Nunc nōn est nox, sed vesper est. Amīcus noster, puer aeger, in casā stat. Ex fenestrā viam spectat. Discipulōs exspectat. Stellās nōn videt quod nōn est nox. Lūna nōn est obscūra. Vesper est. Vesperī agricolae ex agrīs properant et discipulī ex scholā ambulant.

Puer nōn est miser sed laetus. Haec est causa: nunc nōn est aeger. Herī puer erat aeger et in scholā nōn labōrat. Miser erat et sōlus, sed medicus puerō medicīnam dedit et hodiē puer est laetus. Herī puer nōn labōrābat. Impiger nōn erat. Tardus erat. Hodiē est impiger. Scrībit, legit, aquam in casā portat. Herī cum puer medicum vīdit, medicīnam nōn cupiēbat. Medicus, vir benignus, puerō pecūniam dedit, et puer medicīnam cupīvit. Herī puer amīcōs nōn vīdit. Hodiē ex fenestrīs discipulōs in viā videt. Discipulī puerum ex viā vident et ad fenestram properant. Discipulī puerō epistulam dant. Magistra epistulam scrīpsit. Puer epistulam legit et laetus est quod epistula est grāta.
passages.english:
Now it is not night, but evening. Our friend, the sick boy, stands in the house. He looks at the road from the window. He awaits students. He does not see stars because it is not night. The moon is not dark. It is evening. In the evening farmers hurry from the fields and students walk from school.

The boy is not miserable but happy. This is the reason: now he is not sick. Yesterday the boy was sick and he did not work in school. He was miserable and alone, but the doctor gave the boy medicine and today the boy is happy. Yesterday the boy was not working. He was not energetic. He was slow. Today he is energetic. He writes, reads, carries water in the house. Yesterday when the boy saw the doctor, he did not want medicine. The doctor, a kind man, gave money to the boy, and the boy wanted the medicine. Yesterday the boy did not see friends. Today he sees students on the road from the windows. The students see the boy from the road and hurry to the window. The students give the boy a letter. The teacher wrote the letter. The boy reads the letter and is happy because the letter is welcome.
passages.notes: Herī = yesterday; hodiē = today; vesper = evening; vesperī = in the evening. Erat = was (imperfect). Medicīna = medicine.

passages.title: Cap. X · Lūna et Stellae
passages.latin:
Labōrāsne in casā, puella?
Ita. In casā labōrō.
Labōrāsne in casā, puer?
Minimē. Nōn in casā, sed in scholā labōrō.
Labōrātisne in scholā, puerī?
Ita. In scholā labōrāmus, sed nōn in casā. Puellae quoque in scholā labōrant.

Spectāsne lūnam vesperī, puella? Spectāsne stellās noctū, puer?
Noctū stellās spectāmus quod stellās amāmus. Cum lūna est obscūra, stellās vidēmus.

Estne lūna grāta tibi? Suntne stellae tibi grātae?
Ita. Lūnam et stellās amō.

Ubi stās cum stellās spectās?
In casā nōn stō cum stellās specto. Cum in viā stāmus, stellās et lūnam vidēmus.
passages.english:
Do you work in the house, girl? Yes. I work in the house. Do you work in the house, boy? No indeed. I work not in the house, but in school. Do you work in school, boys? Yes. We work in school, but not in the house. The girls also work in school.

Do you watch the moon in the evening, girl? Do you watch the stars at night, boy? At night we watch the stars because we love the stars. When the moon is dark, we see the stars.

Is the moon pleasing to you? Are the stars pleasing to you? Yes. I love the moon and stars.

Where do you stand when you watch the stars? I do not stand in the house when I watch the stars. When we stand on the road, we see the stars and moon.
passages.notes: Labōrātisne? = Do you (pl.) work? Tibi grāta = pleasing to you. Cum + indicative = when. Stō/stāmus = I stand/we stand.

passages.title: Cap. XI · Tabernāculum
passages.latin:
Haec puella parva cum mātre in casā stat. Est aestās et iānua et fenestrae sunt apertae. Vesper est pulcher. Hīc est silva et in silvā tabernāculum vident. In tabernāculō in silvā habitat fēmina. Aestāte fēmina in tabernāculō habitat. Aestāte sōla in tabernāculō manet. Hodiē est in tabernāculō. Herī nōn erat in tabernāculō. Herī erat in oppidō. Saepe ad oppidum ambulat. Ex oppidō multa ad tabernāculum portat. Hodiē erat in silvā. Ex silvā quoque multa portat. Nunc in tabernāculō est et est impigra. Quid nunc habet fēmina? Medicīnam habet. Diū labōrat in tabernāculō et nunc medicīnam habet. Medicīnam ad oppidum portat et pecūniam ad tabernāculum portat. Nōn est medicus sed medicō medicīnam dat. Nōn est aegra fēmina; est valida.

Puella parva cum mātre fēminam et medicīnam spectat. Puella saltat et hoc dīcit: "Laeta sum quod nōn sum aegra. Medicus mihi medicīnam fēminae nōn dat."

Māter respondet: "Ego quoque sum laeta quod es valida et medicīnam nōn cupis. Laeta sum quoque quod in tabernāculō nōn habitāmus. Tēctum magnum nōn habēmus sed cāra mihi est casa nostra."
passages.english:
This little girl stands in the house with her mother. It is summer and the door and windows are open. The evening is beautiful. Here is a forest and in the forest they see a tent. A woman lives in the tent in the forest. In summer the woman lives in the tent. In summer she stays alone in the tent. Today she is in the tent. Yesterday she was not in the tent. Yesterday she was in town. She often walks to town. From town she carries many things to the tent. Today she was in the forest. She also carries many things from the forest. Now she is in the tent and she is diligent. What does the woman have now? She has medicine. She works a long time in the tent and now she has medicine. She carries the medicine to town and carries money to the tent. She is not a doctor but she gives medicine to the doctor. The woman is not sick; she is healthy.

The little girl looks at the woman and the medicine with her mother. The girl jumps and says this: 'I am happy because I am not sick. The doctor does not give me the woman's medicine.'

The mother answers: 'I too am happy because you are healthy and do not want medicine. I am also happy because we do not live in a tent. We do not have a large house but our house is dear to me.'
passages.notes: Aestās/aestāte = summer/in summer. Tabernāculum = tent. Oppidum = town. Valida = healthy/strong. Saltat = jumps/dances. Respondet = answers.

passages.title: Cap. XII · Puer Malus
passages.latin:
Interdum hic discipulus nōn est bonus. Malus est et in scholā nōn bene labōrat. Hodiē magistra nōn est laeta. Maesta est. Puerum nōn laudat. Ubi discipulī scholā domum eunt, magistra ad sē puerum malum vocat et hoc dicit: "Tū, puer, nōn bene labōrās. Nunc tē sōlum labōrāre cupiō. Aquam in scholam portā."

Puer aquam portāre nōn cupit et sēcum, "Domum ire cupiō," inquit. "Hanc magistram nōn amō."

Diū lacrimat. Ūnam hōram puer sōlus cum magistrā manet. Linguam Latīnam discit. Aquam portat. Fenestrās et iānuās claudit. Sed hōra longa nōn est puerō maestō grāta.

Māter puerī domī puerum exspectat et ē fenestrā vocat. "Puer! Puer!" dīcit māter. Puer nōn respondet. Post ūnam hōram māter maesta ē tēctō ad scholam it quod vesper est et puer nōn est domī. Ibi puer stat et lacrimat.

"Cupisne mox bonus esse?" dīcit magistra. "Cupisne bene labōrāre? Ubi bene labōrās tē laudō."

Puer magistram et mātrem maestam spectat. "Ita," respondet puer sed sēcum, "Nunc domum īre cupiō," dīcit.

Mox māter et fīlius domum eunt. Properant quod nox est et stellae et lūna sunt clārae.
passages.english:
Sometimes this student is not good. He is bad and does not work well in school. Today the teacher is not happy. She is sad. She does not praise the boy. When the students go home from school, the teacher calls the bad boy to her and says this: 'You, boy, do not work well. Now I want you to work alone. Carry water into the school.'

The boy does not want to carry water and says to himself, 'I want to go home. I do not love this teacher.'

He cries for a long time. The boy stays alone with the teacher for one hour. He learns the Latin language. He carries water. He closes the windows and doors. But the long hour is not pleasing to the sad boy.

The boy's mother awaits the boy at home and calls from the window. 'Boy! Boy!' says the mother. The boy does not answer. After one hour the sad mother goes from the house to the school because it is evening and the boy is not at home. There the boy stands and cries.

'Do you want to be good soon?' says the teacher. 'Do you want to work well? When you work well I will praise you.'

The boy looks at the teacher and his sad mother. 'Yes,' the boy answers, but to himself he says, 'Now I want to go home.'

Soon the mother and son go home. They hurry because it is night and the stars and moon are bright.
passages.notes: Malus = bad. Maesta = sad. Lacrimat = cries/weeps. Domum = homeward (direction). Domī = at home (location). Sēcum = to himself. Inquit = says/said.
vocab_hints: eunt = they go (from īre, to go)

passages.title: Cap. XIII · Hortus
passages.latin:
Haec casa est domus fēminae benignae. Casa est casa alba. Post casam est hortus. Post hortum est silva. In hortō sunt multī et pulchrī flōrēs. Sunt rosae albae et līlia alba quoque. Alta sunt līlia et pulchrae sunt rosae. Sunt aliī flōrēs quoque in hortō. Grātī fēminae benignae sunt flōrēs. Fēmina hortum nōn cūrat. Vir quī in aliā casā habitat hortum cūrat. Aestāte hortum cūrat; hieme nōn cūrat quod hieme sunt nūllī flōrēs in hortō.

Hodiē vir nōn est in hortō. Herī hīc erat. Hodiē hīc nōn est. Est aestās et in hortō est quiēs. Fēmina quiētem amat.

Mox fēmina puerum lacrimantem audit. Ad iānuam it. Nūllus puer est in viā. Mox puerum quī in hortō labōrat videt. Est fīlius virī quī hortum cūrat. Hodiē puer hortum cūrat et lacrimat.

Fēmina puerum ad sē vocat: "Cūr lacrimās, puer?"

Tum puer respondet: "Pater meus ad oppidum it et ego labōrō. Ego quoque ad oppidum īre cupiō. Duōs equōs habet et ego equōs amō. In stabulō frūmentum equīs dō. Aquam quoque dō. Ūnum equum semper cūrō. Ego quoque cum patre et equīs īre cupiō."

Fēmina, quae est fēmina benigna, est maesta. Fēmina et puer cēnam edunt. Tum fēmina flōrēs ex hortō capit et ad oppidum it. Cum fēminā it puer parvus quī nunc nōn lacrimat.
passages.english:
This house is the home of a kind woman. The house is a white house. Behind the house is a garden. Behind the garden is a forest. In the garden are many beautiful flowers. There are white roses and white lilies too. The lilies are tall and the roses are beautiful. There are other flowers too in the garden. The flowers are pleasing to the kind woman. The woman does not tend the garden. A man who lives in another house tends the garden. In summer he tends the garden; in winter he does not tend it because in winter there are no flowers in the garden.

Today the man is not in the garden. Yesterday he was here. Today he is not here. It is summer and there is quiet in the garden. The woman loves quiet.

Soon the woman hears a crying boy. She goes to the door. No boy is on the road. Soon she sees a boy who is working in the garden. He is the son of the man who tends the garden. Today the boy tends the garden and cries.

The woman calls the boy to her: 'Why are you crying, boy?'

Then the boy answers: 'My father goes to town and I work. I also want to go to town. He has two horses and I love horses. In the stable I give grain to the horses. I give water too. I always tend one horse. I also want to go with my father and the horses.'

The woman, who is a kind woman, is sad. The woman and boy eat dinner. Then the woman takes flowers from the garden and goes to town. The little boy, who no longer cries, goes with the woman.
passages.notes: Hortus = garden. Flōs/flōrēs = flower/flowers. Alba = white. Quiēs = quiet/rest. Equus = horse. Frūmentum = grain. Hiems/hieme = winter/in winter.

passages.title: Cap. XIV · Mārcus et Mīles
passages.latin:
Nox est et quiēs est in terrā. Hiems est. Mārcus, puer Americānus, sōlus est in casā quod māter et pater ad oppidum iērunt. Diū legit. Dē multīs terrīs legit. Hanc fābulam, quae de Rōmānīs antīquīs nārrātur, saepe Mārcus in scholā audīvit. In pictūrā est vir quī mīles Rōmānus est. Ad bellum properat. Est mīles validus et magnus quī arma Rōmāna portat. Multī aliī mīlitēs, quī in pictūrā nōn videntur, ad bellum ambulant quod bellum est magnum. Ibi sunt castra Rōmāna ubi noctū mīlitēs manent.

Hic mīles Rōmānus quī est dux mīlitum arma bona portat. Quae sunt haec arma? Hic est gladius validus. Gladius nōn est longus. Nōn est lātus. Scūtum quoque capit. Scūtum ducem tegit ubi bellum est perīculōsum. Pīlum habet. Hoc pīlum est longum et validum sed nōn est lātum. Galea quoque ducem tegit. Galeam et scūtum et gladium et pīlum Mārcus videt et laudat.

Nunc nōn est nox. Mārcus nōn legit sed ad stabulum ubi equus stat ambulat. In stabulō Mārcus equō albō frūmentum dat. Tum in hortum puer et equus properant. "Ego sum dux Rōmānus," inquit puer, "et arma capiō. Gladium et ūnum scūtum portō. Galeam habeō et in tabernāculō sunt duo pila quae nunc nōn portō. Mīlitēs meī mē laudant quod cōnsilia bona habeō."
passages.english:
It is night and there is quiet on the land. It is winter. Marcus, an American boy, is alone in the house because his mother and father went to town. He reads for a long time. He reads about many lands. Marcus often heard this story, which is told about the ancient Romans, in school. In the picture is a man who is a Roman soldier. He hurries to war. He is a strong and large soldier who carries Roman weapons. Many other soldiers, who are not seen in the picture, walk to war because the war is great. There are Roman camps there where soldiers stay at night.

This Roman soldier who is the leader of soldiers carries good weapons. What are these weapons? Here is a strong sword. The sword is not long. It is not wide. He also takes a shield. The shield protects the leader when the war is dangerous. He has a javelin. This javelin is long and strong but it is not wide. A helmet also protects the leader. Marcus sees and praises the helmet and shield and sword and javelin.

Now it is not night. Marcus is not reading but walks to the stable where the horse stands. In the stable Marcus gives grain to the white horse. Then the boy and horse hurry to the garden. 'I am a Roman leader,' says the boy, 'and I take weapons. I carry a sword and one shield. I have a helmet and in the tent there are two javelins which I do not carry now. My soldiers praise me because I have good plans.'
passages.notes: Arma = weapons. Gladius = sword. Scūtum = shield. Galea = helmet. Pīlum = javelin. Dux = leader/general. Castra = camp (pl.). Bellum = war. Cōnsilia = plans.

passages.title: Cap. XV · Mīles Rōmānus Loquitur
passages.latin:
Colloquium

Quae arma, mīles Rōmāne, habēs?
Galeam et scūtum habeō. Haec est galea mea; hoc est scūtum meum.
Quid facit scūtum?
Scūtum mē tegit.
Quid facit galea?
Galea caput meum tegit.
Quae tēla, mīles Rōmāne, habēs?
Tēla mea sunt gladius et pīlum. Multī mīlitēs sagittās habent sed ego sagittās nōn habeō quod nōn sum sagittārius.

Ubi habitās, mīles Rōmāne?
In castrīs habitō cum est bellum. Castra amō. Castra sunt lāta et longa et multī mīlitēs Rōmānī in castrīs sunt. Longē ā casā patris meī sunt castra. Patrem et mātrem nōn videō. Interdum sum dēfessus et patrem et mātrem vidēre cupiō, sed ad casam nōn eō quod dux hoc dīcit: "Bonus mīles pugnat. Mīles malus domum it."

Cūr cōnsilium nōn capis et domum īs?
Dux meus est benignus dux sed cōnsilium intellegit. Nōn cupit mīlitēs domum īre. "Cōnsilium nōn est bonum," inquit dux. "Est malum. In castrīs manē." Tum numquam īre cupiō.

Labōrāsne semper in castrīs?
Minimē. Ego et trēs amīcī interdum ad silvam īmus. Cum in silvā sumus, iacula habēmus. Sagittās quoque habēmus. Interdum ad castra animālia portāmus.
passages.english:
Conversation

What weapons do you have, Roman soldier? I have a helmet and shield. This is my helmet; this is my shield. What does the shield do? The shield protects me. What does the helmet do? The helmet protects my head. What missiles do you have, Roman soldier? My missiles are a sword and javelin. Many soldiers have arrows but I do not have arrows because I am not an archer.

Where do you live, Roman soldier? I live in camp when there is war. I love the camp. The camp is wide and long and many Roman soldiers are in the camp. The camp is far from my father's house. I do not see my father and mother. Sometimes I am tired and want to see my father and mother, but I do not go to the house because the leader says this: 'A good soldier fights. A bad soldier goes home.'

Why do you not take the plan and go home? My leader is a kind leader but he understands the plan. He does not want soldiers to go home. 'The plan is not good,' says the leader. 'It is bad. Stay in camp.' Then I never want to go.

Do you always work in camp? No indeed. My three friends and I sometimes go to the forest. When we are in the forest, we have javelins. We also have arrows. Sometimes we carry animals to camp.
passages.notes: Tēlum = missile/weapon. Sagittā = arrow. Sagittārius = archer. Pugnat = fights. Dēfessus = tired/weary. Caput = head. Numquam = never. Iaculum = javelin (short spear).
vocab_hints: eō / it = I go / goes (from īre)

passages.title: Cap. XV · Dē Puerīs Quattuor
passages.latin:
Dē Puerīs Quattuor

Quī sunt hī quattuor puerī qui in viā lātā ambulant?
Amīcī sunt et fīliī agricolārum.

Quid faciunt?
Sagittās portant. Scūta et pila nōn portantur quod puerī mīlitēs nōn sunt et nōn pugnant. Nōmen huius puerī altī est Mārcus. Medius puer est. Ā dextrā Mārcī est frāter, Lūcius, puer secundus, quī trēs sagittās portat. Ā sinistrā Mārcī est puer tertius, Carolus, nōmine. In capite Carolī est galea, sed mīles nōn est. Hic parvus puer, cuius nōmen est Claudius, post hōs trēs puerōs manet. Parvus est, sed cum puerīs magnīs ambulāre cupit. Dēfessus est quod puerī magnī properant.

Quid puerī nunc faciunt?
Hī quattuor puerī ā casīs ad silvam properant quod ibi sunt animālia. In stabulō sunt equī nigrī, et nunc ibi manent. Puerī animālia capere cupiunt. Interdum animālia puerōs timent. Sagittās quoque timent. Diū puerī in silvā manent, sed diū animālia nōn videntur.

"Ibi," inquit Mārcus, "caput parvī animālis nigrī videō."

Tum Mārcus prīmam sagittam capit, tum secundam et tertiam. Animal stat et puerum spectat. Nūllum perīculum est quod puerī nōn sunt virī magnī et validī. Puerī domum eunt ubi cēna in mēnsā parāta est. Cēnam laetī edunt.
passages.english:
About Four Boys

Who are these four boys walking on the wide road? They are friends and sons of farmers.

What are they doing? They are carrying arrows. Shields and javelins are not carried because the boys are not soldiers and do not fight. The name of this tall boy is Marcus. He is the middle boy. To the right of Marcus is his brother Lucius, the second boy, who carries three arrows. To the left of Marcus is the third boy, named Carolus. On Carolus' head is a helmet, but he is not a soldier. This small boy, whose name is Claudius, stays behind these three boys. He is small, but he wants to walk with the big boys. He is tired because the big boys hurry.

What are the boys doing now? These four boys hurry from the houses to the forest because there are animals there. In the stable are black horses, and now they remain there. The boys want to catch animals. Sometimes the animals fear the boys. They also fear arrows. The boys stay in the forest for a long time, but for a long time no animals are seen.

'There,' says Marcus, 'I see the head of a small black animal.'

Then Marcus takes the first arrow, then the second and third. The animal stands and looks at the boy. There is no danger because the boys are not big strong men. The boys go home where dinner is prepared on the table. They eat dinner happily.
passages.notes: Nōmen = name. Ā dextrā/sinistrā = to the right/left. Prīmus, secundus, tertius = first, second, third. Mēnsa = table. Parāta = prepared. Timent = fear.
vocab_hints: quī / cuius / huius = who / whose / of this

passages.title: Cap. XVI · Anna in Oppidō
passages.latin:
Haec est agricolae fīlia cuius nōmen est Anna. In casā est. Herī aliquis ad iānuam casae vēnit et epistulam portāvit. Anna iānuam aperuit. Tum epistulam manibus ad mātrem, quae epistulam lēgit, portāvit. Nunc Anna ad oppidum īre parat. Hīc habitat amīca Annae, Iūlia nōmine. Anna ā Iūliā ad oppidum invītātur. Anna est laeta et saltat.

Vesperī puella nōn it. Māne it. Māne in carrō pater et fīlia per agrōs et silvam properant. Equum habent. Equus bene currit quod est validus. Puella est laeta. Ā dextrā et ā sinistrā multa videt. Omnēs rēs sunt grātae. Subitō in agrō quattuor animālia videntur.

Vesperī pater et fīlia in magnō oppidō sunt. Iūlia Annam exspectat et prope fenestram stat. Subitō equum et carrum videt. Ē tēctō currit et "Salvēte" inquit. Tēctum Iūliae est magnum aedificium. Multās fenestrās et quattuor iānuās habet. Ante tēctum est hortus. Per hortum puellae ad iānuam ambulant. Pater, "Valēte," inquit, "puellae," et domum it.

Iūlia trēs amīcās habet. Prīma est fīlia medicī. Secunda est fīlia poētae. Tertia est fīlia mīlitis quī iacula et alia tēla domī habet. In mēnsā stat pictūra virī quī in capite galeam habet.
passages.english:
This is the farmer's daughter whose name is Anna. She is in the house. Yesterday someone came to the door of the house and carried a letter. Anna opened the door. Then she carried the letter with her hands to her mother, who read the letter. Now Anna is preparing to go to town. Here lives Anna's friend, named Julia. Anna is invited to town by Julia. Anna is happy and jumps for joy.

In the evening the girl does not go. She goes in the morning. In the morning the father and daughter hurry through the fields and forest in a wagon. They have a horse. The horse runs well because it is strong. The girl is happy. She sees many things to the right and left. All things are pleasing. Suddenly four animals are seen in the field.

In the evening the father and daughter are in the large town. Julia awaits Anna and stands near the window. Suddenly she sees the horse and wagon. She runs out of the house and says 'Hello!' Julia's house is a large building. It has many windows and four doors. In front of the house is a garden. The girls walk through the garden to the door. The father says 'Goodbye, girls,' and goes home.

Julia has three friends. The first is the daughter of a doctor. The second is the daughter of a poet. The third is the daughter of a soldier who has javelins and other weapons at home. On the table stands a picture of a man who has a helmet on his head.
passages.notes: Māne = in the morning. Carrus = wagon/cart. Subitō = suddenly. Tēctum = house/building (lit. roof). Ante = in front of. Per = through. Aliquis = someone.

passages.title: Cap. XVII · Mīlitēs Eunt
passages.latin:
"Audī. Aliquid audiō. Audīsne aliquid? Multī virī per viās currunt; multī equī veniunt. Sunt carrī quoque et in carrīs multae rēs portantur. Sunt mīlitēs quoque et in manibus mīlitum sunt tēla. Quī sunt hī mīlitēs? Ad quem locum eunt? Vidē. Ibi prope nostram domum veniunt."

Spectāmus et subitō multōs mīlitēs vidēmus. Multī aliī virī trāns viās et ante aedificia currunt quod hōs mīlitēs vidēre cupiunt. Mīlitēs arma et tēla habent. Galeās, gladiōs habent; scūta, pīla, arcūs, sagittās nōn habent. Patria nostra in bellō nōn pugnat, sed fīnitimī nostrī pugnant et nōs sumus sociī. Cum fīnitimī auxilium rogant necesse est īre. Mīlitēs nostrī īre dēbent quod sunt sociī fīnitimōrum.

Dux noster ducī sociōrum cōnsilium dat. Ducem sociōrum monet. Mīlitēs nostrī auxilium fīnitimīs dant quod fīnitimī nōn satis magnās cōpiās habent. Nōs nostrōs mīlitēs tūtōs esse cupimus, sed mīlitēs sociōs nostrōs tūtōs esse cupiunt. Māne hodiē ad sociōs eunt. Multī numquam iterum venient. Multī vītās dabunt, multī morientur.
passages.english:
'Listen. I hear something. Do you hear something? Many men are running through the roads; many horses are coming. There are also wagons and in the wagons many things are carried. There are also soldiers and in the hands of soldiers are weapons. Who are these soldiers? To what place are they going? Look. They are coming near our house.'

We look and suddenly we see many soldiers. Many other men run across the roads and in front of the buildings because they want to see these soldiers. The soldiers have weapons and missiles. They have helmets and swords; they do not have shields, javelins, bows, and arrows. Our homeland does not fight in war, but our neighbors are fighting and we are allies. When the neighbors ask for help it is necessary to go. Our soldiers must go because they are allies of the neighbors.

Our leader gives advice to the leader of the allies. He warns the leader of the allies. Our soldiers give help to the neighbors because the neighbors do not have large enough forces. We want our soldiers to be safe, but the soldiers want our allies to be safe. Today in the morning they go to the allies. Many will never come again. Many will give their lives; many will die.
passages.notes: Fīnitimī = neighbors/neighboring peoples. Sociī = allies. Auxilium = help. Necesse est = it is necessary. Dēbent = must/ought. Cōpiae = forces/troops. Arcus = bow.
vocab_hints: eunt / veniunt = they go / they come

passages.title: Cap. XVIII · Vir Caecus
passages.latin:
Magistra: Quis est ille vir?
Puer: Ille vir est caecus. Ille vir oculōs habet, sed nūllās rēs videt.
Magistra: Cūr adest? Cūr hīc est?
Puer: Ille vir fīlium habet quī prope hunc locum habitat. Vir fīlium vīsitat et nunc fīlium exspectat.
Magistra: Herī ille caecus vir aderat. Nunc iterum adest.
Puer: Ita. Tum fīlium exspectābat et nunc iterum fīlium exspectat. Numquam longē sine fīliō it quod est timidus. Ubi cum fīliō ambulat, tūtus est.
Magistra: Omnēs virī et puerī quoque illī virō auxilium dare dēbent. Sī omnēs auxilium dant, virī caecī sunt laetī neque timent.
Puer: Ecce! Vidē! Fīlius caecī virī adest. Quam laetus est ille vir!
Magistra: Ad quem locum eunt vir et fīlius?
Puer: Necesse est ad aedificium magnum īre quod ibi vir caecus pecūniam accipit.
passages.english: Teacher: Who is that man? Boy: That man is blind. That man has eyes, but sees nothing. Teacher: Why is he here? Why is he here? Boy: That man has a son who lives near this place. The man visits his son and now awaits his son. Teacher: Yesterday that blind man was here. Now he is here again. Boy: Yes. Then he was awaiting his son and now he awaits his son again. He never goes far without his son because he is fearful. When he walks with his son, he is safe. Teacher: All men and boys also ought to give that man help. If all give help, blind men are happy and not afraid. Boy: Look! See! The son of the blind man is here. How happy that man is! Teacher: To what place are the man and son going? Boy: It is necessary to go to the large building because there the blind man receives money.
passages.notes: Caecus = blind. Ille/illa = that (demonstrative). Tūtus = safe. Necesse est + infinitive = it is necessary to. Accipit = receives. Adest = is present/here.
vocab_hints: aderat / adest = was present / is present

passages.title: Cap. XVIII · Miles Caecus
passages.latin:
Magistra: Cūr pecūniam accipit?
Puer: Vir erat mīles et cum sociīs et fīnitimīs prō patriā pugnābat. Prō patriā parātus erat morīrī sī necesse erat. Cōpiae hostium erant magnae sed cōpiae nostrae nōn satis magnae erant. Dux mīlitēs monēbat sed fortēs erant et fortiter pugnābant. Multī vulnerābantur; multī necābantur. Posterō diē aliī mīlitēs illum mīlitem vident, sed numquam posteā ille videt. Hodiē est caecus! "Putābam mē tūtum," inquit. "Eram paene tūtus. Tum tēlum veniēbat et hic sum." Nunc ab illō mīlite ex aedificiō magnō pecūnia accipitur.
passages.english: Teacher: Why does he receive money? Boy: The man was a soldier and was fighting for the homeland with allies and neighbors. He was ready to die for the homeland if it was necessary. The forces of the enemy were great but our forces were not large enough. The leader was warning the soldiers but they were brave and fighting bravely. Many were being wounded; many were being killed. The next day other soldiers see that soldier, but he never sees again after that. Today he is blind! 'I thought myself safe,' he says. 'I was almost safe. Then a weapon was coming and here I am.' Now money is received from that large building by that soldier.
passages.notes: Prō + ablative = for/on behalf of. Hostes = enemies. Vulnerābantur/necābantur = were being wounded/killed (imperfect passive). Posterō diē = the next day. Paene = almost.
vocab_hints: prō + abl. = for, on behalf of
vulnerābantur / necābantur = were being wounded / killed

passages.title: Cap. XIX · Ad Flūmen
passages.latin:
Herī vir caecus mihi fābulam dē bellō in quō prō patriā pugnāvit nārrāvit. Haec est fābula:

Hīc sunt castra nostra. Ibi est flūmen. Trāns flūmen sunt castra hostium. Inter castra hostium et castra nostra illud flūmen fluit. Hodiē hostēs in castrīs manent quod herī multī vulnerātī et necātī sunt.

Ducēs hostium sunt īrātī et mīlitēs noctū castra nostra capere cupiunt. Circum castra nostra est mūrus altus. Sine hōc mūrō perīculum est magnum. Propter hunc mūrum hostēs castra nostra neque capient neque nostrōs vulnerābunt.

Hodiē dux noster adest. Magnās cōpiās postulat. Auxilium postulat accipitque. Mox omnēs rēs erunt parātae quod nocte perīculum erit magnum.

Media nox est. Hostēs exspectāmus.
"Quis oculōs bonōs habet?" rogat dux.
"Ego," respondet mīles quī prope stābat.
"Ad mūrum ī," inquit dux, "et sī hostēs venientēs vidēs, omnēs mīlitēs monē."
"Nunc," clāmat ille mīles, "hostēs in flūmine sunt."
Tum, "Pīla aliaque tēla iacite," dux iubet.

Hostēs cōnsilia esse audīta nōn putābant. Tēla vīdērunt. Posteā ad castra trāns flūmen iērunt.

Posterō diē dux, "Cūr Mārcus, mīles fortis, abest?" inquit. "Herī aderat, sed nunc abest."
Tum omnēs mīlitēs ad flūmen properāvērunt. Ecce! Ibi inter mūrum et flūmen erat Mārcus mortuus. Per caput inter oculōs erat gladius hostis. Bene Mārcus prō patriā pugnāverat.
passages.english:
Yesterday the blind man told me a story about the war in which he fought for the homeland. This is the story:

Here are our camps. There is the river. Across the river are the enemy's camps. That river flows between the enemy's camps and our camps. Today the enemies stay in camp because yesterday many were wounded and killed.

The enemy leaders are angry and the soldiers want to take our camps at night. Around our camps is a high wall. Without this wall the danger is great. Because of this wall the enemies will neither take our camps nor wound our men.

Today our leader is here. He demands large forces. He demands and receives help. Soon all things will be ready because at night the danger will be great.

It is midnight. We await the enemies. 'Who has good eyes?' the leader asks. 'I do,' answers a soldier who was standing nearby. 'Go to the wall,' says the leader, 'and if you see the enemies coming, warn all soldiers.' 'Now,' shouts that soldier, 'the enemies are in the river.' Then the leader orders, 'Throw javelins and other weapons.'

The enemies did not think their plans had been heard. They saw the weapons. Afterwards they went back to camp across the river.

The next day the leader said, 'Why is Marcus, a brave soldier, absent? Yesterday he was here, but now he is absent.' Then all soldiers hurried to the river. Look! There between the wall and the river was Marcus, dead. Through his head between the eyes was the enemy's sword. Marcus had fought well for the homeland.
passages.notes: Flūmen = river. Mūrus = wall. Inter = between. Propter + acc. = because of. Iubet = orders. Mortuus = dead. Abest = is absent.
vocab_hints: vulnerātī et necātī sunt = were wounded and killed
capient / vulnerābunt = they will capture / will wound (future)

passages.title: Cap. XX · Hortus Vāstātus
passages.latin:
Is quī inter mūrum aedificiumque stat est avunculus meus. Māne in hortō est et flōrēs spectat. Nōn est īrātus, sed maestus. Ubi avunculus circumspectat, pulchrās rosās et aliōs flōrēs nōn videt. Undique flōrēs sunt in ruīnīs. Ōlim flōrēs erant pulchrī et ubi avunculus hortum spectābat omnēs probābat. Nunc omnēs flōrēs vāstātī sunt.

Quis hortum vāstābat? Vēnitne aliquis armātus nocte ubi avunculus aberat? Cūr aliquis illum inimicum nōn audiēbat ubi per hortum vēnit? Hoc nōn intellegō.

Quis est is quī ad hortum ex aedificiō properat? Est vir quī avunculum in hortō iuvat. Sine illō auxiliō avunculus hortum nōn bene cūrat quod hortus est magnus. Vir avunculum salūtat.

"Salvē," inquit.
"Salvē," respondet avunculus.

Tum vir quoque circumspectat. Is quoque undique flōrēs in ruīnīs videt.

"Herī," inquit, "prīmus diēs hiemis vēnit et omnia oppugnābat. Quamquam flōrēs pulchrī nōn videntur et eōs nōn probāmus, aestāte, tamen, nūllī erunt in ruīnīs. Hiems est inimīca hortōrum. Hiems bene armāta flōrēs flūminaque oppugnat et semper superat. Interdum validior est quam aestās."

"Ita," respondet avunculus. "Aestās, tamen, est mihī grātior quam hiems."
passages.english:
He who stands between the wall and the building is my uncle. In the morning he is in the garden and looks at the flowers. He is not angry, but sad. When the uncle looks around, he does not see beautiful roses and other flowers. Everywhere the flowers are in ruins. Once the flowers were beautiful and when the uncle looked at the garden he approved of all of them. Now all the flowers are destroyed.

Who was destroying the garden? Did someone armed come at night when the uncle was away? Why did no one hear that enemy when he came through the garden? I do not understand this.

Who is he who hurries to the garden from the building? He is a man who helps the uncle in the garden. Without that help the uncle does not tend the garden well because the garden is large. The man greets the uncle.

'Hello,' he says. 'Hello,' the uncle answers.

Then the man also looks around. He too sees flowers in ruins everywhere.

'Yesterday,' he says, 'the first day of winter came and attacked everything. Although the flowers do not appear beautiful and we do not approve of them, in summer, however, none will be in ruins. Winter is the enemy of gardens. Well-armed winter attacks flowers and rivers and always conquers. Sometimes it is stronger than summer.'

'Yes,' answers the uncle. 'Summer, however, is more pleasing to me than winter.'
passages.notes: Avunculus = uncle. Undique = everywhere. Ōlim = once/formerly. Vāstātus = destroyed/laid waste. Quamquam = although. Tamen = however. Grātior = more pleasing (comparative).
vocab_hints: spectābat / vāstābat / audiēbat = was watching / ruining / hearing (imperfect)

passages.title: Cap. XXI · Mīlitēs Rōmānī
passages.latin:
Nōs sumus mīlitēs Rōmānī. Sumus mīlitēs populī Rōmānī. Illī mīlitēs quōs in illīs castrīs vidētis sunt barbarī. Mox barbarī castra nostra oppugnābunt. Sī nōn hodiē sed crās castra nostra oppugnābunt, nōs tūtī erimus quod sociī nōs iuvābunt et crās sociī aderunt. Sī hodiē oppugnābunt, quamquam multōs mīlitēs nōn habēmus, tamen fortiter et ācriter pugnābimus et barbarī castra nōn expugnābunt. Quamquam ea oppugnābunt, tamen castra nōn expugnābuntur. Castra expugnāre et dēlēre cupiunt sed neque ea expugnābuntur neque dēlēbuntur. Nōs nōn superābimur. Nōs fortēs mīlitēs probābimus.

Mīles putat: "Sī in castrīs manēbō, mox hostēs aderunt. Multī vulnerābuntur; multī necābuntur. Itaque manēre nōn cupiō. Domum īre cupiō. Rūrī habitō. Rūs īre cupiō. Īre nōn dēbeō. Dux est fortis vir. Mīlitēs monet et mē quoque monet. Sī domum ībō, numquam iterum mīles erō. Prō patriā numquam pugnābō, neque posteā laudābor. Quam maestus sum!"

Mīles est in vinculīs. Mīles putat: "Ā castrīs īre nōn debēbam. Ōlim putābam mē īre cupere. Nunc nōn sum timidus, hostēs nōn tīmeō, nūllam rem timeō. Sī dux mē accipiet, quam laetus erō! Dā mihi tēla et arma. Nūllus audit. Sōlus sum. Mē miserum!"
passages.english:
We are Roman soldiers. We are soldiers of the Roman people. Those soldiers whom you see in those camps are barbarians. Soon the barbarians will attack our camps. If they attack our camps not today but tomorrow, we will be safe because allies will help us and tomorrow the allies will be present. If they attack today, although we do not have many soldiers, we will nevertheless fight bravely and fiercely and the barbarians will not storm the camp. Although they will attack it, the camp will not be stormed. They want to storm and destroy the camp but neither will it be stormed nor will it be destroyed. We will not be conquered. We will prove ourselves brave soldiers.

A soldier thinks: 'If I remain in camp, soon the enemies will be here. Many will be wounded; many will be killed. And so I do not want to remain. I want to go home. I live in the country. I want to go to the country. I ought not to go. The leader is a brave man. He warns the soldiers and warns me too. If I go home, I will never be a soldier again. I will never fight for the homeland, and I will not be praised afterward. How sad I am!'

The soldier is in chains. The soldier thinks: 'I ought not to have left camp. Once I thought I wanted to go. Now I am not afraid, I do not fear enemies, I fear nothing. If the leader receives me, how happy I will be! Give me weapons and arms. No one listens. I am alone. Wretched me!'
passages.notes: Barbarus = barbarian. Expugnāre = to storm/capture (by assault). Dēlēre = to destroy. Superābimur = we will be conquered (future passive). Vincula = chains. Rūs/rūrī = countryside/in the country. Ācriter = fiercely.
vocab_hints: erimus / aderunt = we will be / will be here (future)
expugnābuntur / dēlēbuntur / superābimur = will be stormed / destroyed / overcome (future passive)

passages.title: Cap. XXII · Vēnātiō – Pars I
passages.latin:
Ōlim vir et servus per silvam errāvērunt. Servus erat magnus et quod tēla portāvit virum bene iūvit. Laetī erant quod rūs erat grātum eīs et bēstiās ferās capere cupīvērunt. Herī hī duo virī bēstiās ferās esse in silvā audīvērunt. Diū in silvā fuerant et per tōtam silvam errāverant, nam nūllās bēstiās vīderant, et domum sine bēstiīs ferīs īre nōn cupīvērunt.

Māne servus, cuius nōmen erat Carolus, "Sī domum duās bēstiās magnās portābimus," inquit, "magna erit cēna et amīcōs tuōs invītābimus."

"Ita," respondit vir, cuius nōmen erat Mārcus, "facile erit bēstiās oppugnāre et superāre, nam multās esse in silvā scīmus."
passages.english:
Once a man and his slave were wandering through the forest. The slave was large and, because he was carrying weapons, helped the man well. They were happy because the countryside was pleasing to them and they wanted to catch wild beasts. Yesterday these two men had heard that there were wild beasts in the forest. They had been in the forest for a long time and had wandered through the whole forest, for they had seen no beasts, and they did not want to go home without wild beasts.

In the morning the slave, whose name was Carolus, said: "If we carry home two large beasts, the dinner will be great and we will invite your friends."

"Yes," answered the man, whose name was Marcus, "it will be easy to attack and overcome the beasts, for we know that there are many in the forest."
passages.notes: Perfect tense dominates: errāvērunt, fuerant, errāverant, vīderant. Note the pluperfect (fuerant, errāverant, vīderant) showing action completed before another past action. Cuius nōmen erat = 'whose name was' — genitive of possession.

passages.title: Cap. XXII · Equī, Nōn Bēstiae – Pars II
passages.latin:
Sed ubi erat nox et paene tempus domum īre, eī, tamen, sine bēstiīs manēbant. Quamquam lūna stellaeque nunc vidēbantur, nōn erat facile, tamen, viam vidēre. Subitō duo oculī, mox aliī prope virōs videntur. Virī timent. Celeriter bēstiae (nam ita virī putant) ad virōs sē movent. Servus sagittās capit sed timidus est et bēstiae ferae nōn vulnerantur. Tum Mārcus, "Ego," inquit, "vītās nostrās servābō. Celeriter curram et bēstiae post mē venient. Subitō prope flūmen stābō et bēstiae in flūmen current et omnēs morientur. Tūtī erimus."

"Hae bēstiae," inquit Carolus, "vītās nostrās dēlēre cupiunt. Interdum tōtum oppidum expugnant et incolās superant. Bēstiae sunt perīculōsiōrēs quam barbarī."

Proxius bēstiae ferae veniēbant.

"Ecce!" inquit Mārcus. "Bēstiae ferae nōn sunt. Eī sunt equī nostrī quī ē stabulō cucurrērunt. Iānua est aperta. Itaque hīc sunt."

Tum Mārcus ūnum equum cēpit. Carolus ūnum equum cēpit et ubi duo virī eōs incitāvērunt, domum in equīs portātī sunt. Cēterī equī post eōs properāvērunt.
passages.english:
But when it was night and almost time to go home, they remained without beasts, however. Although the moon and stars could now be seen, it was not easy to see the road. Suddenly two eyes, then others, were seen near the men. The men are afraid. Quickly the beasts (for so the men think) move toward the men. The slave takes arrows but is timid and the wild beasts are not wounded. Then Marcus said: "I will save our lives. I will run quickly and the beasts will come after me. Suddenly I will stand near the river and the beasts will run into the river and they will all die. We will be safe."

"These beasts," said Carolus, "want to destroy our lives. Sometimes they storm whole towns and overcome the inhabitants. Beasts are more dangerous than barbarians."

The wild beasts were coming nearer.

"Look!" said Marcus. "They are not wild beasts. They are our horses which ran out of the stable. The door is open. That is why they are here."

Then Marcus took one horse. Carolus took one horse and when the two men urged them on, they were carried home on the horses. The other horses hurried after them.
passages.notes: Passive perfect: vulnerantur (present passive), portātī sunt (perfect passive). Note comparative: perīculōsiōrēs quam = 'more dangerous than.' Future tense sequence: curram, venient, stābō, current, morientur.

passages.title: Cap. XXIII · Servus Perfidus
passages.latin:
Ōlim dominus bonus malum servum habēbat. In multīs locīs dominus servum suum probābat. Prīmum in tēctō suō labōrem dabat, sed servus tēctum eius nōn bene cūrābat neque erat benignus puerīs et puellīs eius. Tum in hortō eius labōrābat sed flōrēs eius nōn amābat neque eōs cūrābat. Tum rūrī in agrīs eius labōrābat sed ibi quoque nōn bene labōrābat.

Mox dominus eius eum ad silvam mīsit. Ā silvīs ad tēctum necesse erat lignum portāre. Servus malus ad silvam īvit sed lignum nōn portāvit. Quamquam dominus imperat, servus nōn pāret. Per tōtum tempus sēcum putat. Cēterōs servōs nōn incitat sed mox sōlus sē in fugam dat. Celeriter currit sed semper timet. Per diēs servus perfidus in agrīs dormit; per noctēs iterum currit.

Mox tamen dominus cum servīs armātīs eum reperit et servus perfidus statim domum in vinculīs it. Ibi dominus eius hoc dīcit: "Tū in tēctō nōn bene labōrās, in hortō nōn bene labōrās, in agrīs nōn bene labōrās. Ad silvam igitur tē mittō. Ibi quoque nōn bene labōrās sed in fugam tē dās. Imperō; nōn pārēs. Quid cupis? Quid in animō habēs?"

"Vōs omnēs necābō," servus perfidus inquit. "Rēs omnēs vestrās dēlēbō."

Dominus igitur multum movētur nam servum saevum esse scit. Statim imperat, "Hunc servum in vincula conicite, servī meī. In locīs obscūrīs post mūrōs validōs semper eum tenēte."
passages.english:
Once a good master had a bad slave. In many places the master tested his slave. First he gave him work in his house, but the slave did not take good care of his house and was not kind to his boys and girls. Then he worked in his garden but did not love his flowers and did not care for them. Then he worked in the country in his fields but there too he did not work well.

Soon the master sent him to the forest. It was necessary to carry wood from the forests to the house. The bad slave went to the forest but did not carry wood. Although the master commands, the slave does not obey. He thinks to himself all the time. He does not urge on the other slaves but soon on his own he takes to flight. He runs quickly but is always afraid. Through the days the treacherous slave sleeps in the fields; through the nights he runs again.

Soon, however, the master finds him with armed slaves and the treacherous slave goes home immediately in chains. There the master says this to him: "You do not work well in the house, in the garden, in the fields. Therefore I send you to the forest. There too you do not work well but take to flight. I command; you do not obey. What do you want? What do you have in mind?"

"I will kill you all," the treacherous slave said. "I will destroy all your things."

The master therefore is much moved for he knows the slave is fierce. He commands immediately: "Throw this slave in chains, my slaves. Keep him always in dark places behind strong walls."
passages.notes: Note mixed tenses — the slave's flight narrated in present (historical present) for vividness. sē in fugam dare = idiom 'to take to flight.' in vinculīs = 'in chains' (ablative of means).

passages.title: Cap. XXIV · Ānserēs Rōmam Servant
passages.latin:
Virī quī Rōmae habitābant Rōmānī vocābantur. Virī quī in Germāniā, in Galliā, in Britanniā habitābant barbarī vocābantur. Quī sunt eī barbarī? Eī barbarī sunt Gallī quī Rōmam sē movent. Rōmānōs nōn amant nam līberī esse cupiunt, sed Rōmānī dominī esse cupiunt et illōs servōs facere cupiunt.

Ānserēs cōnsilium cēpērunt. Dux ānserum cēterōs ānserēs convocāvit et dīxit: "Rēx ānserum sum. Celeriter venīte et audīte. Nōn est tempus morae. Vōbīs rem malam dīcō. Hostēs ad urbem nostram sē movent. Nox est. Nocte oppugnābunt. Ā tergō urbem oppugnābunt. Tōtam urbem dēlēbunt. Neque virōs līberōs neque servōs servābunt, neque hominēs neque bēstiās servābunt. Omnēs necābunt. Tōtōs agrōs vastābunt. Nunc virī Rōmānī sunt dēfessī et dormiunt. Neque vir līber neque servus neque bēstia hostēs videt. Nōs sōlī hanc rem scīmus. Tempus est virōs incitāre. Vōs ānserēs statim clāmōrem facite et virōs incitāte. Rōmam servāte."

Expugnāvēruntne barbarī Rōmam? Minimē, nam ānserēs Rōmam servāvērunt.
passages.english:
The men who lived in Rome were called Romans. The men who lived in Germany, in Gaul, in Britain were called barbarians. Who are those barbarians? Those barbarians are Gauls who are moving toward Rome. They do not love the Romans for they want to be free, but the Romans want to be masters and want to make them slaves.

The geese formed a plan. The leader of the geese called together the other geese and said: "I am king of the geese. Come quickly and listen. There is no time for delay. I tell you a bad thing. The enemy is moving toward our city. It is night. They will attack by night. They will attack the city from the rear. They will destroy the whole city. They will save neither free men nor slaves, neither men nor animals. They will kill all. They will lay waste all the fields. Now the Roman men are weary and are sleeping. Neither free man nor slave nor animal sees the enemy. We alone know this matter. It is time to rouse the men. You geese, make a clamor immediately and rouse the men. Save Rome."

Did the barbarians storm Rome? Not at all, for the geese saved Rome.
passages.notes: Historical reference to the Gallic invasion of Rome (390 BC). Note future tense in speech: oppugnābunt, dēlēbunt, necābunt, vastābunt. Minimē = 'Not at all' (strong negative answer).
vocab_hints: clāmōrem faciō = to raise a shout
minimē = not at all, by no means

passages.title: Cap. XXV · Vīta Ad Flūmen – Pars I
passages.latin:
In silvīs ad quās aestāte īmus est flūmen parvum. Hoc flūmen in flūmen magnum fluit. In flūmine magnō sunt multae et magnae nāvēs quae hominēs et rēs portant. Semper nāve ad urbem quae est prope casam nostram īmus. Haec nāvis est longa et angusta et alba. Nāvem amō quod est tam pulchra et tam celeriter it.

Ōlim ego et pater ex urbe ad casam per flūmen parvum in nāviculā īvimus. Id ego amāvī, sed frāter meus nōn amāvit quod eum cum mātre et sorōribus īre necesse erat. Cum in silvā sumus, nōs omnēs in aquam sine morā īmus. Per tōtum diem in flūmine sumus et magnī clāmōrēs ibi audīrī possunt.

In flūmine est saxum magnum, paene īnsula. Ad hoc saxum ego per aquam ambulāre possum. Ubi prope rīpam sum, tōtum corpus meum vidēre potes. Mox tergum, nōn corpus vidēs. Ubi prope saxum sum, caput meum sōlum vidērī potest. Aqua igitur nōn est alta, sed in flūmine natāre possum.
passages.english:
In the forests to which we go in summer there is a small river. This river flows into a large river. In the large river there are many large ships which carry people and goods. We always go by boat to the city which is near our cottage. This ship is long and narrow and white. I love the ship because it is so beautiful and goes so quickly.

Once my father and I went from the city to the cottage through the small river in a small boat. I loved that, but my brother did not love it because it was necessary for him to go with his mother and sisters. When we are in the forest, we all go into the water without delay. Through the whole day we are in the river and loud shouts can be heard there.

In the river there is a large rock, almost an island. I can walk through the water to this rock. When I am near the bank, you can see my whole body. Soon you see my back, not my body. When I am near the rock, only my head can be seen. The water therefore is not deep, but I can swim in the river.
passages.notes: First-person narrator. fluō / fluit = 'to flow.' Passive infinitive: audīrī possunt = 'can be heard.' paene = 'almost, nearly.'

passages.title: Cap. XXV · Nāvicula Ānser – Pars II
passages.latin:
Interdum per tōtum diem aut natō aut in saxō sedeō aut in rīpā lēgō. Nāviculam quoque habeō. Nōmen nāviculae est "Ānser." Sorōrēs meae et frāter meus quoque natant. Sorōrēs sunt puellae magnae et sē servāre possunt, sed frāter meus est parvus. Bene natat. Paucī puerī tam bene natant, sed sī etiam prope rīpam ambulat, caput nōn vidēs.

Frāter autem nōn est validus, itaque, sī est in flūmine, numquam eum relinquō. Sī nōn natāre cupiō, in saxō sedeō et puer in aquā natat aut tēcta in rīpā aedificat. Hanc vītam amō quod sum tam līber. "Ego sum rēx hominum," interdum inquam. "Haec vīta sōla est bona."

Heu! Ubi hiems venit miser sum quod flūmen relinquō et nōn semper manēre possum. Semper hunc locum memoriā teneō.
passages.english:
Sometimes through the whole day I either swim or sit on the rock or read on the bank. I also have a small boat. The name of the little boat is "Goose." My sisters and my brother also swim. The sisters are big girls and can look after themselves, but my brother is small. He swims well. Few boys swim so well, but even if he walks near the bank, you cannot see his head.

My brother, however, is not strong; therefore, if he is in the river, I never leave him. If I do not want to swim, I sit on the rock and the boy swims in the water or builds houses on the bank. I love this life because I am so free. "I am king of men," I sometimes say. "This life alone is good."

Alas! When winter comes I am miserable because I leave the river and cannot always stay. I always hold this place in my memory.
passages.notes: Note aut … aut = 'either … or.' memoriā teneō = 'I hold in memory.' Hiems = winter. The boat named Ānser connects back to the geese of Cap. XXIV.
vocab_hints: memoriā teneō = to hold in memory, remember

passages.title: Cap. XXVI · Sōlus In Nāviculā – Pars I
passages.latin:
Herī sōlus in nāviculā meā in flūmine eram. Māne ubi lūx nōn iam erat obscūra domum relīquī et ad rīpam flūminis properāvī. Hīc nāviculam parvam angustamque vīdī. Diēs erat pulcher. Nūllae nūbēs erant in caelō. Avēs laetae ē somnō excitātae in arboribus sedēbant. Undique erant collēs. In summīs collibus erant agricolae. Iam labōrābant.

Diū silēbam. Dē nātūrā locī putābam. Collēs, arborēs, caelum quoque silēbant, sed avēs nōn silēbant. In arboribus avēs audiēbam. Itaque laetus eram. Aliae tēcta sua aedificābant, aliae cibum dēsīderābant, aliae ubi appropinquāvī per caelum properābant.

Inter collēs, per silvās in nāviculā īvī. Posteā corpus meum erat dēfessum et cibum cupīvī. Prope rīpam saxum magnum vīdī. Ibi sēdī et diū quiētem cēpī. Tum in flūmine natāvī. Ubi natāre nōn iam cupīvī, in nāviculā meā sēdī.

Subitō post lignum tergum hominis vīdī. Sine morā rīpae appropinquāvī et ibi agricolam vīdī. Dēfessus erat et quiētem capere cupīvit.

"Salvē," dīxī.

"Salvē," respondit agricola.

"Cuius ager est hic?" rogāvī.

"Meus ager est," respondit vir.
passages.english:
Yesterday I was alone in my small boat on the river. In the morning when the light was no longer dark I left home and hurried to the bank of the river. Here I saw the small narrow boat. The day was beautiful. There were no clouds in the sky. Happy birds, awakened from sleep, sat in the trees. Everywhere there were hills. On the tops of the hills were farmers. They were already working.

For a long time I was silent. I was thinking about the nature of the place. The hills, the trees, the sky too were silent, but the birds were not silent. I heard birds in the trees. So I was happy. Some were building their nests, others were seeking food, others hurried through the sky as I drew near.

Among the hills, through the forests I went in my boat. Afterward my body was weary and I wanted food. Near the bank I saw a large rock. There I sat and rested for a long time. Then I swam in the river. When I no longer wanted to swim, I sat in my little boat.

Suddenly beyond a piece of wood I saw the back of a man. Without delay I drew near to the bank and there I saw a farmer. He was weary and wanted to rest.

"Hello," I said.

"Hello," answered the farmer.

"Whose field is this?" I asked.

"This field is mine," answered the man.
passages.notes: Nūbēs = clouds (3rd declension). aliae … aliae … aliae = 'some … others … others.' quiētem capiō = idiomatic 'to rest.'

passages.title: Cap. XXVI · Fābula Agricolae – Pars II
passages.latin:
Tum dē agrīs, dē nātūrā eius locī, dē collibus cārīs suīs nārrāvit. Haec erat eius fābula:

"Ubi parvus puer eram, pater meus māterque in carrō per agrōs ībant. Illīs temporibus nōn erat facile trāns terram, Americam, īre quod multa erant perīcula. Equī erant tardī et saepe necesse erat diū in ūnō locō manēre, ubi aut virī dēfessī aut fēminae aegrae erant. Interdum paucōs diēs nūllum cibum habēbant. Sī flūmina erant magna, necesse erat diū in rīpā manēre. Tum virī nāvēs aedificābant aut sī nāvēs aedificāre nōn poterant, equōs cum carrīs per flūmen dūcēbant."

"Ōlim barbarī appropinquāvērunt. Māter paterque perīculum esse magnum sciēbant et diū post collem, inter arborēs manēbant. Diū silēbant et sine cibō manēbant quod barbarī per noctem in castrīs prope collem manēbant. Māne, prīmā lūce, pater et māter ā collibus properāvērunt."

Cum hanc fābulam audieram, hoc dīxī: "Pater tuus est exemplum virī fortis. Hodiē nōn sunt tam multa exempla virōrum fortium."

"Ita," respondit vir. "Etiam nunc in Americā sunt multī et fortēs virī. Semper erunt."
passages.english:
Then he told about the fields, the nature of his place, his dear hills. This was his story:

"When I was a small boy, my father and mother were going across the fields in a wagon. In those times it was not easy to travel across the land, America, because there were many dangers. The horses were slow and often it was necessary to stay a long time in one place, where either the men were weary or the women were sick. Sometimes for a few days they had no food. If the rivers were large, it was necessary to stay for a long time on the bank. Then the men built boats, or if they could not build boats, they led the horses with wagons through the river."

"Once barbarians drew near. The mother and father knew that the danger was great and they stayed for a long time behind a hill, among the trees. They were silent for a long time and stayed without food because the barbarians remained through the night in a camp near the hill. In the morning, at first light, the father and mother hurried away from the hills."

When I had heard this story, I said: "Your father is an example of a brave man. Today there are not so many examples of brave men."

"Yes," answered the man. "Even now in America there are many brave men. There always will be."
passages.notes: Cum + pluperfect (cum hanc fābulam audieram) = 'when I had heard.' Exemplum virī fortis = 'an example of a brave man' — genitive of description.
