vocab_hints: prīmā lūce = at first light, at dawn

passages.title: Cap. XXVII · In Summō Saxō – Pars I
passages.latin:
Herī in summō saxō stetī et terram undique spectāvī. Caelum erat clārum pulchrumque. Nūllae nūbēs erant. Ubīque collēs arborēsque vīdī. Avēs erant laetae. Ubīque signa laetitiae vidēbantur. Flūmen erat prope et paucās nāviculās vīdī. Subitō figūrās duōrum virōrum vīdī. Virī cibum tēlaque portābant. Splendida erant tēla. Virī in saxō sedēbant sed mē hīc esse nōn sentiēbant. Ego, igitur, post arborem mē cēlāvī et audīvī. Diū silēbam.

Vir altior hoc dīxit: "Ōlim in hōc saxō erat proelium magnum et multī barbarī moriēbantur. Per tōtam terram hoc proelium est nōtum."

"Cūr barbarī hostēs nōn superāvērunt? Habēbantne nūlla tēla?" rogāvit amīcus eius. "Cūr tēla nōn rapuērunt et hostēs oppugnāvērunt?"
passages.english:
Yesterday I stood on the top of the rock and looked at the land on all sides. The sky was clear and beautiful. There were no clouds. I saw hills and trees everywhere. The birds were happy. Everywhere signs of joy were visible. The river was nearby and I saw a few small boats. Suddenly I saw the shapes of two men. The men were carrying food and weapons. The weapons were splendid. The men were sitting on the rock but they did not notice that I was here. Therefore I hid myself behind a tree and listened. I was silent for a long time.

The taller man said this: "Once on this rock there was a great battle and many barbarians were dying. Through the whole land this battle is well known."

"Why did the barbarians not overcome the enemy? Did they have no weapons?" asked his companion. "Why didn't they grab weapons and attack the enemy?"
passages.notes: signa laetitiae = 'signs of joy' — genitive. Vir altior = 'the taller man' (comparative used as noun). mē cēlāvī = reflexive 'I hid myself.'
vocab_hints: cēlō / mē cēlō = to hide / to hide oneself

passages.title: Cap. XXVII · Proelium In Saxō – Pars II
passages.latin:
"Hostēs circum tōtum saxum mittēbantur," inquit vir altior, "et barbarī ā saxō ad tēcta sua īre nōn poterant. Multōs diēs in summō saxō barbarī sine cibō manēbant. Iam multī erant aegrī. Mox undique erant corpora mortua. Posteā omnēs barbarī superātī sunt. Sīc nōmen huius locī nōtum erat. Hoc proelium est exemplum multōrum et dūrōrum proeliōrum quae inter barbarōs et Americānōs facta."

Nunc, excitātus, mē nōn iam cēlāvī, sed virīs appropinquāvī. "Tōtam fābulam," inquam, "audīvī et est mihi grāta. Aliās nārrā."

Vir altior ita respondit: "Lūx est obscūra et tempus est domum īre." Tum vir et amīcus eius tēla splendida capiēbant et dē summō saxō properābant.

Diū figūrās eōrum spectābam. Diū ibi manēbam. Nunc hīc erat nūllum proelium. Omnēs rēs silēbant. Laetitiam sentiēbam, sed miser quoque eram. Etiam nunc fābulam dē nātūrā huius proelī in animō meō teneō.
passages.english:
"The enemy was being sent around the whole rock," said the taller man, "and the barbarians could not go from the rock to their homes. For many days the barbarians remained on top of the rock without food. Already many were sick. Soon dead bodies were everywhere. Afterward all the barbarians were overcome. Thus the name of this place was well known. This battle is an example of many hard battles which were fought between barbarians and Americans."

Now, excited, I no longer hid myself, but I drew near to the men. "I have heard the whole story," I said, "and it is pleasing to me. Tell other stories."

The taller man answered thus: "The light is dim and it is time to go home." Then the man and his companion were taking their splendid weapons and hurrying down from the top of the rock.

For a long time I watched their shapes. For a long time I stayed there. Now there was no battle here. All things were silent. I felt happiness, but I was sad too. Even now I hold the story of the nature of this battle in my mind.
passages.notes: Passive imperfect: mittēbantur = 'were being sent.' corpus mortuum = 'dead body.' fābulam in animō teneō = 'I hold the story in my mind.'
vocab_hints: animō teneō = to hold in mind, remember

passages.title: Cap. XXVIII · Cursus Nāvigiōrum
passages.latin:
Hic est vir cuius fāma nōn cēlātur. Cīvis oppidī nostrī est. Ubīque nōtus est. Ā multīs cīvibus laudātur. Nāvigium pulchrum quod nunc in portū est habet. Nāvigium nōn est angustum sed lātum et longum. In summō corpore est figūra avīs albae. Prope figūram Avis Alba scrīptum est. Hoc est īnsigne nāvigī. Sīc nōtum est inter alia nāvigia.

Prīmā lūce, hodiē, multī cīvēs ad portum vēnērunt. Caelum est clārum. Ubīque sunt nāvigia splendida. Hūc illūc nautae currunt et omnia parant. Prīmō signum datur et omnia nāvigia portum relinquunt; deinde per aquam celeriter eunt. Nunc nāvigium cīvis nostrī est secundum. Nautae, tamen, nōn sunt ignāvī. Nunc alia nāvigia superāre cōnstituunt. Nāvigium cīvis nostrī celerius it.

In summā nāvī cīvis stat et alia nāvigia spectat. Nautīs celeriōrem viam mōnstrat et sīc nautīs imperat: "Heu! Etiam nunc aliud nāvigium ante nōs it. Properātē, nautae!"

Ecce! Prīmum nāvigium tardius it. Saxum nōn videt et nunc īre nōn potest. Tandem Avis Alba est prīma. Cīvēs quī prope portum stant inter sē hās rēs dīcunt: "Cīvis noster praemium accipit. Mox ad aliās gentēs ībit et alia praemia accipiet. Aliae gentēs cīvem nostrum laudābunt."
passages.english:
Here is a man whose fame is not hidden. He is a citizen of our town. He is known everywhere. He is praised by many citizens. He has a beautiful boat which is now in the harbor. The boat is not narrow but wide and long. On the top of the body there is a figure of a white bird. Near the figure White Bird is written. This is the emblem of the boat. Thus it is known among the other boats.

At first light today many citizens came to the harbor. The sky is clear. Everywhere there are splendid boats. This way and that way sailors run and prepare everything. First a signal is given and all the boats leave the harbor; then they go quickly through the water. Now the citizen's boat is second. The sailors, however, are not lazy. Now they resolve to overcome the other boats. The citizen's boat goes more quickly.

On top of the boat the citizen stands and watches the other boats. He shows the sailors a faster route and commands the sailors: "Hey! Even now another boat goes before us. Hurry, sailors!"

Look! The first boat goes more slowly. It does not see the rock and now it cannot go. At last White Bird is first. The citizens who stand near the harbor say these things among themselves: "Our citizen receives the prize. Soon he will go to other nations and will receive other prizes. Other nations will praise our citizen."
passages.notes: Present passive throughout: cēlātur, laudātur, datur, excitantur. Hūc illūc = 'here and there.' secundus = 'second.' īnsigne = emblem, insignia.

passages.title: Cap. XXVIII · Urbs Mea
passages.latin:
Ōlim locus ubi urbs mea stat erat silva. Bēstiae et barbarī errābant per silvās et trāns flūmina. Tum virī Gallicī hūc vēnērunt. Signa pulchra portābant; splendida īnsignia galeās et scūta ōrnābant. Barbarī nōn erant ignāvī sed virī Gallicī novīs armīs armātī erant. Erant multa proelia, tamen. Diū pugnātum est. Tandem barbarī castra virōrum oppugnāvērunt. Cōpia cibī nōn erat satis magna et virī perterritī erant. Saepe colloquia habēbant et tandem cōnstituērunt barbarōs oppugnāre. Hoc fēcērunt et sīc in fugam barbarōs dedērunt. Virī Gallicī tamen nōn mānsērunt. Castra mōvērunt et ad flūmen magnum īvērunt.

Deinde virī Britannicī vēnērunt et aedificāvērunt castra quae mānsērunt. Prīmō erat oppidum parvum. In portū tamen erant multa nāvigia et multī hīc labōrābant. Multī virī ad hōc oppidum īvērunt et mox erat urbs. Nunc est urbs magna et etiam nunc virī hūc veniunt et manent.

Hī virī quī vēnērunt erant validī et multa intellegēbant. Viās longās et lātās fēcērunt. Illī virī tēcta magna et pulchra aedificāvērunt. Cīvēs bonōs tamen habēmus et tandem hī cīvēs bonī excitābuntur et nōs illam gentem malam ex urbe nostrā magnā cum laetitiā mittēmus.
passages.english:
Once the place where my city stands was a forest. Wild animals and barbarians wandered through the forests and across rivers. Then French men came here. They carried beautiful standards; splendid emblems adorned helmets and shields. The barbarians were not lazy but the French men were armed with new weapons. There were many battles, however. The fighting went on for a long time. At last the barbarians attacked the camp of the men. The supply of food was not large enough and the men were terrified. They often held councils and at last resolved to attack the barbarians. They did this and thus put the barbarians to flight. The French men, however, did not stay. They moved camp and went to the great river.

Then British men came and built a camp which endured. At first it was a small town. In the harbor, however, there were many ships and many worked here. Many men went to this town and soon there was a city. Now it is a great city and even now men come here and stay.

The men who came were strong and understood many things. They made long wide roads. Those men built great and beautiful buildings. We have good citizens, however, and at last these good citizens will be aroused and with great joy we will send that bad people out of our city.
passages.notes: Gallicī = 'French', Britannicī = 'British.' pugnātum est = impersonal passive 'there was fighting.' cōpia cibī = 'supply of food' — genitive.
vocab_hints: pugnātum est = there was fighting (impersonal passive)

passages.title: Cap. XXIX · Nūntius Ad Exercitum
passages.latin:
Exercitus noster erat in castrīs. Māne erat. Vigilēs erant in moenibus. Undique prōspectābant. Mox vigil mīlitem in equō portātum vidēbat. Mīles celeriter veniēbat. Deinde clāmor factus est. Mīlitēs properāvērunt. Mox galeae erant in capitibus, scūta et gladiī erant parāta, pīla erant in manibus. Dux est prope portam. Tandem mīles appropinquat. Insignia quae galeam ōrnant mōnstrant eum amīcum esse. Hīc est nūntius quī ex fīnitimā gente venit. Hūc venit quod gēns est perterrita. Cīvēs eius gentis nōn sunt ignāvī sed barbarī veniunt et cīvēs sine sociīs nōn pugnāre audent. Est flūmen prope urbem et barbarī nāvigia nōn habent. Haec est causa morae parvae. Sed mox ad urbem venient.

Dux noster excitātur. Paucōs mīlitēs vocat. Mīlitēs vocātī veniunt et colloquium habent. Tandem cōnstituunt exercitum mittere. Dux partem exercitūs nostrī sēcum dūcere cōnstituit. Partem in castrīs relinquit. Circum moenia castrōrum mīlitēs vāllum aedificābunt. Vāllum aedificātum castra validiōra faciet. Exercitus et dux arma rapiunt. Mox sunt parātī. Agmen faciunt. In prīmō agmine sunt dux et nūntius. Deinde agmen sē movet.
passages.english:
Our army was in camp. It was morning. Watchmen were on the walls. They were looking out on all sides. Soon a watchman saw a soldier being carried on a horse. The soldier was coming quickly. Then a shout arose. The soldiers hurried. Soon helmets were on heads, shields and swords were ready, javelins were in hands. The general is near the gate. At last the soldier draws near. The emblems which adorn his helmet show that he is a friend. This is the messenger who comes from a neighboring people. He comes here because the people is terrified. The citizens of that people are not cowardly but barbarians are coming and the citizens do not dare fight without allies. There is a river near the city and the barbarians have no boats. This is the cause of the short delay. But soon they will come to the city.

Our general is aroused. He calls a few soldiers. The summoned soldiers come and hold a council. At last they resolve to send the army. The general resolves to lead part of our army with himself. He leaves part in camp. Around the walls of the camp soldiers will build a rampart. The rampart, once built, will make the camp stronger. The army and general seize weapons. Soon they are ready. They form a column. At the head of the column are the general and the messenger. Then the column moves.
passages.notes: Rich military vocabulary: vigil, moenia, galeae, scūta, gladiī, pīla, vāllum, agmen. agmen = 'column of march.' vāllum aedificātum = 'the rampart, once built' — perfect passive participle.
vocab_hints: moenia = walls (of a city or camp)

passages.title: Cap. XXX · Nūntiī – Pars I
passages.latin:
Herī ego et Mārcus erāmus nūntiī. Epistulam ā patre meō ad fīnitimum portāvimus. Iter erat longum. Per agrōs, trāns flūmen, ad montēs ubi fīnitimus habitābat īvimus. Pars itineris erat grāta. Ubi, autem, dēfessī erāmus iter nōn facere cōnstituimus. Diū prope montem parvum sēdimus. Tum quiētem in hōc locō idōneō cēpimus.

Subitō agmen exercitūs magnī vīdī. Ex portīs moenium veniēbat. Agmen erat longum et ad hunc montem veniēbat. Auxilium vocāre nōn poteram quod nōn validus eram, et clāmōrēs facere nōn poteram. Dux exercitūs hoc dīxit: "Hic locus est idōneus castrīs. Hīc manēbimus. Vallum facite et, vigilēs, prōspectāte. Undique sunt hostēs."

Perterritus nunc eram et mēcum hoc dīxī: "Dux mē nōn videt. Arbor mē cēlat. Silēbō et ille mē nōn vidēbit. Nocte montem relinquam et mox tūtus domī erō."

Subitō dux appropinquāvit et mē vīdit. "Ecce! Hic, mīlitēs, nōn est cīvis noster. Nōn est Etrūscus. Rōmānus est. In illā urbe habitat. Urbs appellātur Rōma. Illam urbem occupāre cupimus. In illam impetum faciēmus. Vītam huius puerī nōn servābimus. Illum ad summum montem portāte et ibi corpus eius relinquite."
passages.english:
Yesterday Marcus and I were messengers. We carried a letter from my father to a neighbor. The journey was long. We went through fields, across a river, to the mountains where the neighbor lived. Part of the journey was pleasant. When, however, we were weary we resolved not to travel. We sat for a long time near a small mountain. Then we rested in this suitable place.

Suddenly I saw the column of a large army. It was coming from the gates of walls. The column was long and was coming toward this mountain. I could not call for help because I was not strong, and I could not raise shouts. The general of the army said this: "This place is suitable for a camp. We will stay here. Make a rampart and, watchmen, look out on all sides. Everywhere there are enemies."

Now I was terrified and said this to myself: "The general does not see me. The tree hides me. I will be silent and he will not see me. At night I will leave the mountain and soon I will be safe at home."

Suddenly the general drew near and saw me. "Look! Here, soldiers, is not one of our citizens. He is not an Etruscan. He is a Roman. He lives in that city. The city is called Rome. We want to occupy that city. We will make an attack on it. We will not save this boy's life. Carry him to the top of the mountain and leave his body there."
passages.notes: The narrator is a Roman boy caught by Etruscans. idōneus castrīs = 'suitable for a camp' (dative after adjective). appellātur = 'is called' (present passive). Etrūscī = Etruscans.
vocab_hints: impetum faciō = to make an attack

passages.title: Cap. XXX · Horātius – Pars II
passages.latin:
Dux ipse gladium rapuit. Subitō ex ulteriōre monte clāmōrēs audītī sunt. Signa exercitūs Rōmānī vidēbantur. Magnam laetitiam sēnsī.

"Deī mē servābunt," dīxī, "quī ōlim Rōmam ab hostibus servābant."

Etrūscī perterritī mē relīquērunt et arma parāvērunt. Clāmōrēs eōrum mē excitāvērunt. Undique circumspectāvī sed nūlla signa vīdī. Nūlla castra, nūllī hostēs, nūllus dux erat prope.

"Ubi sumus?" rogāvī.

Mārcus quoque excitātus est. Eī omnia nārrāvī. Tum epistulam vīdimus. Tempus erat currere quod necesse erat epistulam fīnitimō ante noctem dare.

"Vīdistīne fortem Horātium?" rogāvit Mārcus. "Ōlim Rōma virtūte Horātiī servāta est. Deī auxilium suum dedērunt. Virtūs huius Rōmānī per multās terrās nōta est."

"Hoc sciō," respondī, "quod herī fābulam dē proeliīs quae inter Etrūscōs Rōmānōsque pugnāta sunt legī."

Mox erat fīnis itineris et ubi epistulam fīnitimus accēperat, domum īvimus.
passages.english:
The general himself seized his sword. Suddenly shouts were heard from the more distant mountain. The standards of the Roman army were visible. I felt great joy.

"The gods will save me," I said, "who once saved Rome from its enemies."

The terrified Etruscans left me and prepared their weapons. Their shouts roused me. I looked around on all sides but saw no standards. There were no camps, no enemies, no general nearby.

"Where are we?" I asked.

Marcus was also roused. I told him everything. Then we saw the letter. It was time to run because it was necessary to give the letter to the neighbor before nightfall.

"Did you see brave Horatius?" asked Marcus. "Once Rome was saved by the virtue of Horatius. The gods gave their help. The virtue of this Roman is known through many lands."

"I know this," I answered, "because yesterday I read the story about the battles which were fought between the Etruscans and Romans."

Soon was the end of the journey and when the neighbor had received the letter, we went home.
passages.notes: The Etruscan scene was a dream! virtūte Horātiī = 'by the virtue of Horatius' — ablative of means + genitive. Reference to Horatius Cocles. Vīdistīne = Vīdistī + -ne.
vocab_hints: Horātius = Horatius Cocles (hero of the bridge)
pugnāta sunt = were fought (neuter pl. perfect passive)
fīnis itineris = end of the journey

passages.title: Cap. XXXI · Exercitus Rōmānus
passages.latin:
Exercitus imperātōrem ūnum habet. Sunt multī ducēs. Imperātor imperat; ducēs pārent. Deinde ducēs imperant et reliquī mīlitēs pārent. In exercitū sunt multae legiōnēs. Circiter tria mīlia mīlitum "legiō" appellantur. Īdem imperātor omnēs legiōnēs quae in exercitū sunt dūcit, sed ūna legiō ūnum ducem habet; itaque in exercitū sunt multī ducēs.

Imperātor bonus semper hostēs vincit quod mīlitēs eum amant et laetī eunt ubi dūcit. Semper bonus imperātor castra in locō idōneō pōnit et numquam mīlitēs in locum inīquum dūcit. Imperātor ipse nōn est deus, sed est vir quī virtūtem multam habet et multa scit et multa putat. Sī perīculum est magnum, imperātor ipse mīlitēs dūcit. Tum mīlitēs fortiter pugnant et vincunt.

Ōlim erat malus imperātor. Castra nōn in summō monte posuit sed sub monte. Trāns flūmen quod prope castra erat in ulteriōre rīpā erant hostēs fortēs. Bonus imperātor hōs hostēs dūxit. Cōpiās suās dīvīsit. Aliae legiōnēs in castrīs mānsērunt. Aliās legiōnēs trāns flūmen circum montem ad summum montem dūxit et hae subitō dē monte cucurrērunt. Per nivem cucurrērunt. Exercitum hostium vīcērunt et castra expugnāvērunt. Multa mīlia mīlitum necāta sunt et reliquī sē in fugam dedērunt. Ab eōdem imperātōre numquam iterum ille exercitus ductus est quod imperātor bonus nōn erat.
passages.english:
The army has one commander. There are many generals. The commander commands; the generals obey. Then the generals command and the rest of the soldiers obey. In the army there are many legions. About three thousand soldiers are called a 'legion.' The same commander leads all the legions which are in the army, but one legion has one general; therefore in the army there are many generals.

A good commander always overcomes the enemy because the soldiers love him and go gladly wherever he leads. A good commander always places the camp in a suitable place and never leads soldiers into an unfavorable place. The commander himself is not a god, but is a man who has great virtue and knows many things and thinks about many things. If the danger is great, the commander himself leads the soldiers. Then the soldiers fight bravely and conquer.

Once there was a bad commander. He placed the camp not on top of the mountain but below the mountain. Across the river which was near the camp on the farther bank were strong enemies. A good commander led these enemies. He divided his forces. Some legions remained in camp. He led other legions across the river around the mountain to the top of the mountain and these suddenly ran down from the mountain. They ran through the snow. They overcame the army of the enemy and stormed the camp. Many thousands of soldiers were killed and the rest took to flight. By the same commander that army was never led again because he was not a good commander.
passages.notes: Roman army organization: imperātor > ducēs > legiō > centuriō. Circiter tria mīlia = 'about three thousand' — mīlia is neuter plural, takes genitive. nix, nivis, f. = snow.
vocab_hints: cōpiās dīvīdō = to divide one's forces

passages.title: Cap. XXXII · Lēgātus Captus
passages.latin:
Legiō est pars exercitūs sed legiō ipsa in multās partēs dīvīsa est. Imperātor tōtum exercitum dūcit; lēgātus legiōnem dūcit. Vir quī ūnam ex partibus legiōnis dūcit est centuriō. Temporibus antīquīs illa pars quam centuriō dūxit circiter centum mīlitēs habēbat.

Ōlim lēgātus et centuriō in eōdem proeliō pugnābant. Proelium erat grave quod locus ubi legiō stābat erat inīquus. Hostēs quī erant in locō idōneō, in summīs montibus, exspectābant. Post legiōnem et ante legiōnem erant hostēs. In locō inīquō inter montēs manēbat lēgātus et cum mīlitibus exspectābat.

Tandem lēgātus graviter vulnerātus est et ab hostibus captus est. Sed comes lēgātī, centuriō bonus, legiōnem ad proelium novum excitāvit.

"Necesse est lēgātum nostrum ab hostibus servāre," inquit centuriō. "Ego nocte ad castra hostium modō agricolae ībō. Tum hostēs ad castra nostra prōcēdent et vōs eritis parātī. Celeriter oppugnāte."

Sōlus agricolae modō centuriō ad castra inimīca prōcessit. Imperātor hostium fābulam centuriōnis audīvit et cum circiter mīlle mīlitibus ē castrīs ad legiōnem iter fēcit. Lēgātus et comes eius ab hostibus nōn vīsī aliā viā properāvērunt.

Iam legiō omnia parāverat et ad hostēs prōcessit. Subitō legiō sub monte oppugnāvit et hostēs nōn parātī vīctī sunt. Posterō diē lēgātus centuriōnem ad sē vocāvit. Tum eī praemium dedit. Posteā cīvēs centuriōnem magnā cum laetitiā excēpērunt. Praemium erat corōna centuriōnī grāta. In caput eius posita est.
passages.english:
A legion is part of the army but the legion itself is divided into many parts. The commander leads the whole army; the legate leads the legion. The man who leads one of the parts of the legion is a centurion. In ancient times the part which the centurion led had about one hundred soldiers.

Once a legate and a centurion were fighting in the same battle. The battle was serious because the place where the legion stood was unfavorable. The enemy who were in a suitable place, on the tops of mountains, was waiting. Behind the legion and before the legion were enemies. In the unfavorable place between the mountains the legate remained and waited with the soldiers.

At last the legate was seriously wounded and was captured by the enemy. But the companion of the legate, a good centurion, roused the legion to a new battle.

"It is necessary to save our legate from the enemy," said the centurion. "I will go by night to the enemy's camp in the manner of a farmer. Then the enemy will advance toward our camp and you will be ready. Attack quickly."

Alone in the manner of a farmer the centurion advanced to the enemy's camp. The commander of the enemy heard the centurion's story and made the march from camp to the legion with about a thousand soldiers. The legate and his companion, unseen by the enemy, hurried by another way.

Already the legion had prepared everything and advanced toward the enemy. Suddenly the legion attacked below the mountain and the unprepared enemy was defeated. The next day the legate called the centurion to himself. Then he gave him a prize. Afterward the citizens received the centurion with great joy. The prize was a crown pleasing to the centurion. It was placed on his head.
passages.notes: lēgātus = legate, commander of a legion. The centurion infiltrates disguised as a farmer. Posterō diē = 'on the following day.' corōna = crown or wreath — Roman military decoration.
vocab_hints: modō + genitive = in the manner of
posterō diē = on the following day

passages.title: Cap. XXXIII · Latrōnēs
passages.latin:
Ōlim ad tēctum magnum prope Rōmam vēnērunt latrōnēs. Hī latrōnēs erant saevī et praedam, maximē pecūniam, cupīvērunt. Omnēs quī sē dēfendērunt interficere voluērunt. Hastās tulērunt quās iēcērunt. Hīs multōs graviter vulnerāvērunt et omnēs eōs timuērunt. Hī latrōnēs centuriōnem habuērunt; fuērunt enim circiter centum latrōnēs.

Ubi ad hoc tēctum vēnērunt, dominus servōs ad sē vocāvit et gladiīs et pilīs et hastīs et saxīs latrōnēs excēpērunt. Fortiter omnēs tēctum dēfendērunt. Paene bellum gessērunt. Dominus erat imperātor, nam lēgātus in exercitū Rōmānō fuerat et bellum bene scīvit. Cum paucīs comitibus inter latrōnēs prōcessit et gladiō suō multōs interfēcit. Comitēs eius erant fortēs quod ipse fortis erat.

"Is servus quī centuriōnem latrōnum interficiet," inquit dominus, "corōnam habēbit et eum līberum faciam. Sī cīvitās latrōnēs nōn vincere potest, nōs ipsī eōs expellēmus."

Multī servī sē fortēs probāvērunt et quīdam centuriōnem vulnerāvit sed nēmō eum interfēcit. Ubi latrōnēs discessērunt, dominus servōs convocāvit.

"Vōs laudāre volō," inquit dominus, "bene enim hoc bellum gessistis. Celeriter latrōnēs expulistis. Bonī servī estis. Crās cēnam magnam habēbitis."
passages.english:
Once robbers came to a large house near Rome. These robbers were fierce and wanted plunder, especially money. They wanted to kill all who defended themselves. They carried spears which they hurled. With these they seriously wounded many and all feared them. These robbers had a centurion; for there were about a hundred robbers.

When they came to this house, the master called the slaves to himself and they received the robbers with swords and javelins and spears and rocks. All defended the house bravely. They almost waged war. The master was a commander, for he had been a legate in the Roman army and understood war well. He advanced among the robbers with a few companions and killed many with his sword. His companions were brave because he himself was brave.

"The slave who kills the centurion of the robbers," said the master, "will have a crown and I will make him free. If the state cannot overcome the robbers, we ourselves will drive them out."

Many slaves proved themselves brave and someone wounded the centurion but no one killed him. When the robbers departed, the master assembled the slaves.

"I want to praise you," said the master, "for you waged this war well. You quickly drove out the robbers. You are good slaves. Tomorrow you will have a great dinner."
passages.notes: latrō / latrōnēs = robber(s). Note offer of freedom for the slave who kills the robber leader. gessistis = 'you waged' — perfect of gerō. bellum gerō = 'to wage war' (idiom).
vocab_hints: līber faciam = I will make free

passages.title: Cap. XXXIV · Prope Mare – Pars I
passages.latin:
Herī prope mare iacēbāmus, ego et Cornēlius, comes meus. In grāmine iacēbāmus. Pulchrum erat grāmen. Nūllum sonitum hominum audiēbāmus. Sonitum sōlum maris audiēbāmus. Umbra arboris erat nōbīs grāta quod aestās erat et post iter longum dēfessī erāmus. Ubīque in terrā erat pāx. Sed pāx numquam est in marī. Mare semper sē movet. Mare spectābāmus. Nēminem vidēbāmus, quamquam nāvēs vidēre poterāmus.

Mox nōs somnō gravī dabāmus. Hoc nōn erat difficile. Subitō sonitum pedum audīvī. Undique per grāmen circumspectāvī. Ut animālia hostēs suōs spectant, sīc ego per grāmen hostēs meōs spectāvī. Sub umbrā saxī latrōnem vīdī et mox prope saxum aliī, comitēs eius, vidēbantur. Hastās ferēbant. Mē armīs dēfendere nōn poteram quod nūlla arma tuleram. Ā saxō latrōnēs expellere volēbam sed nōn sōlus hoc facere poteram.

Comitem meum vocāvī sed Cornēlius ipse discesserat. Auxilium maximē volēbam. Latrōnēs ab hōc locō expellere volēbam.

"Sī cum latrōnibus bellum geram," ut putābam, "bellum sine hastā atque gladiō gerētur. In tantō perīculō esse nōn cupiō."
passages.english:
Yesterday we were lying near the sea, I and Cornelius, my companion. We were lying in the grass. The grass was beautiful. We heard no sound of men. We heard only the sound of the sea. The shadow of the tree was pleasing to us because it was summer and after a long journey we were weary. Everywhere on land there was peace. But peace is never on the sea. The sea always moves. We were watching the sea. We saw no one, although we were able to see ships.

Soon we were giving ourselves to deep sleep. This was not difficult. Suddenly I heard the sound of feet. I looked around through the grass on all sides. Just as animals look for their enemies, so I looked through the grass for my enemies. Under the shadow of the rock I saw a robber and soon near the rock others, his companions, were visible. They were carrying spears. I was not able to defend myself with weapons because I had brought no weapons. I wanted to drive the robbers from the rock but I was not able to do this alone.

I called my companion but Cornelius himself had gone away. I very much wanted help. I wanted to drive the robbers from this place.

"If I should wage war with the robbers," as I was thinking, "war would be waged without spear or sword. I do not want to be in so great a danger."
passages.notes: Simile: ut animālia … sīc ego = 'just as animals … so I.' sonus pedum = 'sound of feet.' somnō mē dō = 'to give oneself to sleep.'
vocab_hints: somnō mē dō = to give oneself to sleep

passages.title: Cap. XXXIV · Somnium – Pars II
passages.latin:
Latrōnēs bene mūnītī sunt. Ante saxum erat mare. Post saxum rīpa altissima illum locum mūniēbat. Difficile atque perīculōsum erat latrōnibus appropinquāre. Diū exspectāvī. Nocte saxō appropinquāvī et latrōnēs praedam spectantēs vīdī. Nēminem sonitum pedum meōrum audīre volēbam. In grāmine in rīpā post saxum iacēbam. Ita enim vidērī nōn poteram. Verba virōrum audīvī. Cōnsilium eōrum audīvī. Tēctum patris meī oppugnāre cōnstituēbant. Tum maximē timēbam. Tantum erat perīculum.

"Necesse est," inquam, "patrem monēre. Nūntius erō." Sed pedēs meī movērī nōn poterant. Subitō oculī meī apertī erant. In somnum mē dederam. Ubīque erat pāx. Hīc Cornēlius quoque iacēbat. Oculī eius quoque apertī erant.

"Cūr, amīce," inquit, "tantam cūram in oculīs tuīs videō?"

Tōtam fābulam nārrāvī. Posteā ad saxum errāvimus sed ibi nēmō erat. Mox tempus erat domum īre. Celeriter properāvimus.
passages.english:
The robbers were well fortified. Before the rock was the sea. Behind the rock the highest bank fortified that place. It was difficult and dangerous to approach the robbers. I waited for a long time. At night I drew near to the rock and saw the robbers watching their plunder. I did not want anyone to hear the sound of my feet. I was lying in the grass on the bank behind the rock. For thus I could not be seen. I heard the words of the men. I heard their plan. They were resolving to attack my father's house. Then I was very afraid. So great was the danger.

"It is necessary," I said, "to warn my father. I will be the messenger." But my feet could not be moved. Suddenly my eyes were open. I had given myself to sleep. Everywhere there was peace. Here Cornelius also lay. His eyes too were open.

"Why, friend," he said, "do I see so great a worry in your eyes?"

I told the whole story. Afterward we wandered to the rock but no one was there. Soon it was time to go home. We hurried quickly.
passages.notes: Another dream sequence. pedēs movērī nōn poterant = 'feet could not be moved' — passive infinitive with possum. cūra in oculīs = 'worry in the eyes' — vivid Latin idiom.
vocab_hints: vidērī nōn poteram = I could not be seen (passive)

passages.title: Cap. XXXV · Rēs Pūblica Rōmāna
passages.latin:
Diū Rōmae rēgēs erant. Rēx omnia fēcit. Erat imperātor exercituum et omnēs rēxit. Imperium summum habēbat. Mox tamen rēx nōn bene imperium gessit et officium neglēxit et populum pressit. Populus erat īrātus et statim rēgem expulit. Deinde cīvitās, rēs pūblica, facta est et duo cōnsulēs imperium habēbant.

Erant aliī magistrātūs quoque. Quaestor pecūniam gessit. Sī umquam magistrātus officium bene gessit, populus huic magistrātuī aliud et altius officium dedit. Sī erat malus magistrātus et populum pressit, numquam iterum officium habuit. Fēlīx est ea cīvitās quae nūllōs nisi bonōs magistrātūs habet!

Hic cōnsul est vir saevus. Officium neglegit, urbem neglegit. In urbe est neque grāmen neque flōrēs neque arborēs quae umbram dant. Nova et pulchra aedificia nōn aedificat. Nēminem nisi sē amat. Urbem nōn mūnit; sī hostēs venient, facile erit urbem expugnāre. Rēs, quās cōnsul facit, sunt mālae. Imperium sōlum sibi vult. Populum sub pedibus suīs premit.

Cūr populus nōn hunc cōnsulem expellit? Populus sē in somnum dedit. Ut virī quī dormiunt, nihil faciunt. Hic cōnsul est hostis pūblicus. Auxilium date, cīvēs. Hunc cōnsulem malum ex urbe expellite.
passages.english:
For a long time there were kings in Rome. The king did everything. He was the commander of the armies and ruled everyone. He had supreme power. But soon the king did not manage his authority well and neglected his duty and pressed down upon the people. The people were angry and immediately expelled the king. Then a state, the Republic, was formed and two consuls held power.

There were other magistrates too. The quaestor managed money. If ever a magistrate performed his duty well, the people gave this magistrate another and higher position. If he was a bad magistrate and pressed down upon the people, he never held office again. Happy is that state which has only good magistrates!

This consul is a fierce man. He neglects his duty, he neglects the city. In the city there is neither grass nor flowers nor trees which give shade. He does not build new and beautiful buildings. He loves no one but himself. He does not fortify the city; if enemies come, it will be easy to storm the city. The things the consul does are bad. He wants power for himself alone. He presses the people under his feet.

Why does the people not expel this consul? The people gave itself to sleep. Like men who are sleeping, they do nothing. This consul is a public enemy. Give help, citizens. Expel this bad consul from the city.
passages.notes: Rome's transition from monarchy to republic (509 BC). rēs pūblica, cōnsul, magistrātus, quaestor = key civics vocabulary. imperium gerō = 'to manage authority.' hostis pūblicus = 'public enemy' — formal Roman legal term.
vocab_hints: cōnsul = consul (one of two annual leaders)
officium neglegō = to neglect one's duty

passages.title: Cap. XXXVI · Centuriō Et Hostēs
passages.latin:
Ōlim quīdam mīlitēs castellum tenuērunt quod erat prope pontem quī erat in flūmine magnō. In castellō erat neque ūllus magistrātus reī pūblicae neque dux quī magnum imperium habuit. Erat nūllus dux nisi centuriō. Centuriō tamen erat fortis mīles et sī umquam perīculum aderat, centuriō officium suum nōn neglēxit.

Hōc tempore centuriō scīvit hostēs appropinquāre per explōrātōrēs quōs mīserat. Explōrātōrēs celeriter centuriōnem petīvērunt. "Magnī numerī hostium veniunt," inquiunt. "Mox aderunt et nōs graviter prement. Semper fēlīcēs sunt; itaque semper per multōs annōs in proeliīs vīcērunt et nōs sumus paucī."

Centuriō paucōs mīlitēs habuit. Virtūtem autem hostium cognōvit sed, quod fortūna fortēs iuvat, pugnāre fortiter cōnstituit. Suās cōpiās in duās partēs dīvīsit. Alteram partem intrā moenia manēre iussit. Alteram partem post arborēs longē ā castellō latēre iussit. Ubi hostēs, circiter septem mīlia mīlitum, vēnērunt, cum comitibus quī intrā castellum erant, centuriō celeriter ex castellō cucurrit, hostēs statim oppugnāvit, eōs reppulit.

Ut hī hostēs discēdēbant, mīlitēs quī latēbant subitō eōs oppugnāvērunt. Eōdem tempore ex utrīsque partibus oppugnātī sunt. Multī mīlitēs centuriōnis reppulērunt, deinde per fugam salūtem repperērunt, sed multī interfectī sunt.

Ubi hostēs discessērunt, utraque pars mīlitum ad castellum īvit. Centuriō omnēs laudāvit et quod omnibus salūtem tulit omnēs sēnsērunt centuriōnem bene regere.
passages.english:
Once some soldiers held a small fort which was near a bridge in a large river. In the fort there was neither any magistrate of the republic nor a general who held great authority. There was no general except the centurion. The centurion however was a brave soldier and if ever danger was present, the centurion did not neglect his duty.

At this time the centurion learned that the enemy was approaching through scouts whom he had sent. The scouts quickly sought the centurion. "Large numbers of the enemy are coming," they said. "Soon they will be here and will press us hard. They are always successful; and so for many years they have always won in battles and we are few."

The centurion had few soldiers. But he learned the strength of the enemy and, because fortune helps the brave, he resolved to fight bravely. He divided his forces into two parts. He ordered one part to remain inside the walls. He ordered the other part to hide behind trees far from the fort. When the enemy, about seven thousand soldiers, came, with companions who were inside the fort, the centurion quickly ran out of the fort, immediately attacked the enemy, drove them back.

As these enemies were withdrawing, the soldiers who were hiding suddenly attacked them. At the same time they were attacked from both sides. Many soldiers drove back the centurion's men, then found safety in flight, but many were killed.

When the enemies had departed, both parts of the soldiers went to the fort. The centurion praised all and because he brought safety to all, all felt that the centurion ruled well.
passages.notes: Classic ambush strategy: divide forces, lure enemy, attack from both sides. fortūna fortēs iuvat = famous proverb (Virgil). explōrātōres = scouts. utrīsque partibus = 'from both sides.'
vocab_hints: fortūna fortēs iuvat = fortune favors the brave
ex utrīsque partibus = from both sides

passages.title: Cap. XXXVII · Exsul – Pars I
passages.latin:
Haec est īnsula ubi exsul paene sōlus habitat. Amīcōs suōs nōn videt. Quandō est hostis, exsul est et hic exsul numquam iterum patriam vidēbit. Septem annīs anteā in hunc locum pervēnit. Magistrātūs patriae, virī magnā vī, exsulem domum venīre nōn sinunt quod ōlim ipse hostem patriam intrāre sinēbat. Tum erat victōria hostium quī diū intrā fīnēs patriae manēbant. Hic vir erat nōn sapiēns at perfidus. Cīvēs sapientēs salūtem patriae cupiunt. Nunc exsul nihil habet. Patriam, amīcōs, domum, patrem, mātrem nōn videt. Semper, autem, sē iterum domum itūrum esse spērat. In numerō hostium cīvitāte habētur.

Hōc modō vir exsul factus est: Ōlim patria diū cum hostibus pugnāverat. Uterque exercitus, bellō dēfessus, pācem cupiēbat. Mīlitēs officia neglegere et domum īre paene parātī erant, sed uterque exercitus victōriam cupiēbat.

"Quō modō pāx esse potest sine victōriā?" rogāvit magistrātus maximus. Mīlitēs vōcem magistrātūs audiēbant et diūtius in bellō manēbant. Paucī mīlitēs, autem, magistrātūs patriae nōn laudābant. Apud eōs erat exsul, explōrātor bonus, quī interdum cōnsilia hostium cognōscēbat.

"Quō tempore erit fīnis bellī?" explōrātor cum aliīs mīlitibus inquit. "Nisi mox fīnis erit, salūtem fugā petam."
passages.english:
This is the island where the exile lives almost alone. He does not see his friends. When he is an enemy, he is an exile and this exile will never see his homeland again. Seven years ago he arrived at this place. The magistrates of his homeland, men of great power, do not allow the exile to come home because he once allowed the enemy to enter his homeland. Then there was a victory of the enemy who remained for a long time within the borders of the homeland. This man was not wise but treacherous. Wise citizens desire the safety of the homeland. Now the exile has nothing. He does not see his homeland, friends, home, father, mother. He always hopes, however, that he will go home again. He is reckoned in the number of enemies by the state.

This is how the man became an exile: Once the homeland had fought for a long time with enemies. Each army, weary of war, desired peace. The soldiers were almost ready to neglect their duties and go home, but each army desired victory.

"In what way can there be peace without victory?" asked the highest magistrate. The soldiers heard the voice of the magistrate and remained longer in the war. A few soldiers, however, did not praise the magistrates of the homeland. Among them was the exile, a good scout, who sometimes learned the plans of the enemy.

"When will there be an end to the war?" the scout said with other soldiers. "Unless there will be an end soon, I will seek safety in flight."
passages.notes: Exile as punishment for treason. exsul = 'exile' (noun). sinō + accusative + infinitive = 'to allow someone to.' in numerō hostium habētur = 'is reckoned in the number of enemies.' diūtius = 'longer' (comparative of diū).
vocab_hints: in numerō habētur = is counted among

passages.title: Cap. XXXVII · Prōditiō – Pars II
passages.latin:
Paucīs diēbus posteā explōrātor ē castrīs it. Flūmen exercitum explōrātōris ab alterō exercitū dīvīsit. Trāns pontem properāvit et noctū in castellum hostium pervēnit. Scūtum et galeam et alia tēla hostium portāvit et nōn cognōscēbātur. Mox ille et ūnus ex hostium explōrātōribus erant amīcī. Uterque bellō dēfessus pācem petīvit. Mox cōnsilium cēperant.

"Pecūniam tibi dabō," inquit hostis, "sī cōnsilia exercitūs tuī nārrābis. Quantam pecūniam postulās?"

Posterā nocte duo explōrātōrēs diū prope pontem latēbant. Exercitus hostium oppugnāre erat parātus. Cōnsilia exercitūs patriae cognita erant. Mox tēla coniciēbantur et prīmō mīlitēs patriae repellēbantur, sed victōria hostium nōn erat longa. Ducēs mīlitum patriae dē explōrātōre perfidō iam cognōvit et cōnsilia nova cēpērunt. Multās hōrās erat pāx inter duōs exercitūs. Exercitus patriae tandem iterum multa tēla coniciēbat et hōc modō hostēs, quī nihil hōrum cōnsiliōrum cognōverat, superātī sunt.

"Quandō hic explōrātor perfidus fuit, eum capiēmus et ad īnsulam mittēmus," inquiunt cīvēs post victōriam. "Eum in patriā manēre sinere nōn possumus. Exsul erit et longē ā patriā habitābit. Nisi exsul erit, hīc vīta eius in perīculō erit."

Itaque hic vir sōlus in īnsulā manet et in numerō hostium habētur.
passages.english:
A few days later the scout left camp. The river divided the scout's army from the other army. He hurried across the bridge and arrived at night at the enemy's fort. He was carrying the shield and helmet and other weapons of the enemy and was not recognized. Soon he and one of the enemy's scouts were friends. Each worn out by war sought peace. Soon they had formed a plan.

"I will give you money," said the enemy, "if you will tell the plans of your army. How much money do you demand?"

The following night two scouts were hiding for a long time near the bridge. The enemy's army was ready to attack. The plans of the homeland's army had been learned. Soon weapons were being hurled and at first the soldiers of the homeland were being driven back, but the victory of the enemy was not long. The leaders of the homeland's soldiers now learned about the treacherous scout and made new plans. For many hours there was peace between the two armies. The homeland's army at last again was hurling many weapons and in this way the enemy, who had learned nothing of these plans, was overcome.

"When this treacherous scout is caught, we will capture him and send him to an island," say the citizens after the victory. "We cannot allow him to remain in the homeland. He will be an exile and will live far from his homeland. Unless he is an exile, his life here will be in danger."

And so this man remains alone on the island and is reckoned in the number of enemies.
passages.notes: Traitor-scout ends in exile. nōn cognōscēbātur = 'he was not recognized' — imperfect passive. Quantam pecūniam postulās? — quantus in questions. noctū = at night (adverb).
vocab_hints: nōn cognōscēbātur = was not recognized (imperfect passive)

passages.title: Cap. XXXVIII · Duo Frātrēs – Pars I
passages.latin:
Ōlim erant duo frātrēs. Alter erat vir magnā vī. Sapiēns erat et amīcus fīdus nōbilisque, at ā frātre nōn amābātur. Agricola erat. Diū dīligenterque labōrāverat et ā fīnitimīs maximē amābātur. At alter frāter, vir perfidus et inīquus, neque tēctum habēbat neque ab amīcīs amābātur. Frātrem quī agrōs et multōs equōs et tēctum et fīliōs fīliāsque habēbat is vir numquam laudābat. Omnēs hās rēs frāter perfidus dēsīderābat et ipse nōn habēbat. Vir perfidus erat servus. Rēs dominī, autem, nōn dīligenter cūrābat.

Ōlim autem, ubi multī agricolae servīque in oppidō conveniēbant, hī duo frātrēs quoque ibi erant. Alter, vir sapiēns, alterum salūtāvit. At servus nōn respondit et īrātus vidēbātur. Posteā, ubi frāter sapiēns sērō ab oppidō redībat, sōlus per silvam ambulāre coepit. Subitō vōcem audīvit. Sē vertit at nihil vīdit. Iterum ambulāre coepit. Usque ad pontem pervēnerat. Subitō in cōnspectū erat figūra frātris eius, servī.

"Sī flūmen trānsīre audēbis," inquit, "tē occīdam. Mihi pecūniam dā. Quantam tēcum habēs?"

Cōnspectus frātris eius nōn erat grātus agricolae, eius tēla domī erant. Sēcum hoc dīxit: "Quō modō mē dēfendam? Quantum est perīculum! Quandō iterum in locō tūtō erō?"
passages.english:
Once there were two brothers. One was a man of great strength. He was wise and a faithful and noble friend, but he was not loved by his brother. He was a farmer. He had worked for a long time and diligently and was greatly loved by his neighbors. But the other brother, a treacherous and unjust man, had neither a home nor was loved by friends. This man never praised his brother who had fields and many horses and a house and sons and daughters. The treacherous brother desired all these things and did not have them himself. The treacherous man was a slave. He did not, however, take careful care of his master's things.

Once however, when many farmers and slaves were meeting in the town, these two brothers were also there. One, the wise man, greeted the other. But the slave did not answer and seemed angry. Afterward, when the wise brother was returning late from town, he began to walk alone through the forest. Suddenly he heard a voice. He turned around but saw nothing. He began to walk again. He had arrived as far as the bridge. Suddenly the shape of his brother, the slave, was in view.

"If you dare cross the river," he said, "I will kill you. Give me money. How much do you have with you?"

The appearance of his brother was not pleasing to the farmer — his weapons were at home. He said this to himself: "How shall I defend myself? How great is the danger! When will I again be in a safe place?"
passages.notes: alter … alter = 'one … the other' (of two). usque ad = 'all the way to.' coepit + infinitive = 'began to.' sēcum = 'to himself.'
vocab_hints: alter … alter = one … the other (of two)
usque ad = all the way to, up to

passages.title: Cap. XXXVIII · Reconciliātiō – Pars II
passages.latin:
Tum vir sapiēns frātrī tōtam pecūniam dedit et, "Pecūniam tibi dō," inquit. "Nōlī mē occīdere. Mē domum redīre sine. Es frāter meus et tibi auxilium dabō."

Sed hae rēs nōn erant grātae servō quī tēlum subitō cēpit et impetum facere coepit. Agricola, autem, sē vertit et ubi saxum reppererat hoc in caput frātris coniēcit. Servus vulnerātus at nōn occīsus est. Victōria erat agricolae. Sērō domum redībat frātrem vulnerātum portāns.

Post multōs diēs servus iam validus, "Ad dominum meum," inquit, "redīre nōn cupiō, at tēcum manēre. Mihi vītam bonam mōnstrāvistī. Tū es amīcus meus. Numquam iterum mē tibi perfidum futūrum esse spērō."

Agricola tandem frātrem manēre sinēbat. Nōn iam erat servus exsul ā tēctō frātris suī et usque ad fīnem vītae servus bonus erat.
passages.english:
Then the wise man gave all his money to his brother and said: "I give you the money. Do not kill me. Allow me to return home. You are my brother and I will give you help."

But these things were not pleasing to the slave, who suddenly took a weapon and began to make an attack. The farmer, however, turned around and when he had found a rock threw it at his brother's head. The slave was wounded but not killed. The victory was the farmer's. He was returning home late carrying his wounded brother.

After many days when the slave was now strong: "I do not want to return to my master," he said, "but to stay with you. You have shown me a good life. You are my friend. I hope that I will never again be treacherous to you."

The farmer at last allowed his brother to stay. No longer was the slave an exile from his brother's house and he was a good slave to the end of his life.
passages.notes: Reconciliation and redemption. Nōlī + infinitive = 'Don't!' (negative imperative). frātrem vulnerātum portāns = 'carrying his wounded brother' — present participle. usque ad fīnem vītae = 'to the end of his life.'
vocab_hints: usque ad fīnem vītae = to the end of life
spērō … futūrum esse = I hope … that (it) will be

passages.title: Cap. XXXIX · Nocturnae Vigiliae
passages.latin:
"Per multōs annōs centuriō fuī. Anteā fuī mīles quī cum aliīs mīlitibus pugnābam. Saepe eīs diēbus eram vigil. Vigilēs vesperī in moenibus dispōnēbantur. Nihil in bellō tam necesse est quam vigilēs bonī. Nox in quattuor partēs 'vigiliās' vocātās dīviditur. Per omnēs vigiliās vigilēs prōspectant. Sī hostēs vīsī sunt, vigilēs clāmōribus magnīs comitēs incitant. Omnēs tum tēla omnī genere sūmunt et hostēs repellunt. Multās noctēs prīmī vigilēs prīmā vigiliā conveniunt, per tōta moenia dispōnuntur, usque ad vigiliam secundam manent; tum aliī hōs succēdunt, hīs deinde ab aliīs tertiā vigiliā succēditur et sīc nox it, sed hostis nūllus vidētur."

"Sī nocte hostēs castra oppugnant, semper magnā vī impetum faciunt. Sī possunt, mīlitēs ā moenibus pellunt, ad moenia currunt, portās frangunt. Secūrēs sēcum portant et nōn gladiīs sed secūribus portās caedunt. Deinde gladiīs et tēlīs omnī genere pugnant. Intereā etiam omnēs, quī sunt in cōnspectū, secūribus occīdunt. Numquam terga vertunt sī homō aut praeda ūllō genere est relicta."

"Ad tēctum meum mēcum venī. Apud mē est secūris quam in quōdam proeliō cēpī. Hanc secūrim ex locō sūmam et eam tibi ostendam. Mēcum venī."

Laetus ego, puer parvus, cum nōbilī virō iī. Laetus secūrim spectāvī. Diū mānsī et fābulās audīvī. Bella antīqua intellegere coepī. Sērō tēctum relīquī et domum meam rediī.
passages.english:
"For many years I was a centurion. Before that I was a soldier who fought with the other soldiers. Often in those days I was a watchman. In the evening the watchmen were stationed on the walls. Nothing in war is so necessary as good watchmen. The night is divided into four parts called 'watches.' Through all the watches the watchmen look out. If enemies are seen, the watchmen rouse their companions with loud shouts. All then take up weapons of every kind and drive back the enemy. Many nights the first watchmen gather at the first watch, are stationed throughout all the walls, remain until the second watch; then others succeed them, then by others at the third watch these are relieved, and so the night goes, but no enemy is seen."

"If the enemy attacks the camp at night, they always make an attack with great force. If they can, they drive the soldiers from the walls, run to the walls, break down the gates. They carry axes with them and cut down the gates not with swords but with axes. Then they fight with swords and weapons of every kind. Meanwhile they also kill with axes all who are in sight. They never turn their backs if any person or plunder of any kind is left."

"Come with me to my house. At my house there is an axe which I took in a certain battle. I will take this axe from its place and show it to you. Come with me."

Happy I, a small boy, went with the noble man. Happily I looked at the axe. I stayed for a long time and heard stories. I began to understand ancient wars. Late I left the house and returned to my own home.
passages.notes: Four Roman night-watches (vigiliā). secūris = axe (3rd declension, irregular: secūrim in accusative). tergum vertō = 'to turn one's back' — idiom for fleeing. succēdō + dative = 'to relieve, succeed.'
vocab_hints: tergum vertō = to turn one's back, flee

passages.title: Cap. XL · Prō Lībertāte
passages.latin:
Prō comitibus suīs fortis eques stetit et hoc dīxit: "Nōs sumus cīvēs quī in rēgnō habitāmus. Rēx quī nōn hīc habitat imperium in hōc rēgnō habet. Nōs rēgī omnia quae petit damus et multōs annōs dedimus. Omnia imperāta rēgis facimus. Intereā quid rēx nōbīs dat? Quid recipimus ex rēge? Honōremne an iniūriam recipimus? Utrum vult rēx? Pācem? An bellum? Quid nōs facere dēbēmus? Quid facere possumus? Apud mē sunt tēla. Haec tēla sūmam. Vōs quoque tēla capite. Nōlīte ad loca eōrum ea restituere. Prō cīvibus usque ad fīnem vītae pugnābimus!"

Ex illō locō exiērunt illī cīvēs et magnā vī pugnāre coepērunt. Tum bellum grave inceptum est. Castella et castra rēgis cīvēs circumvēnērunt et ignibus dēlēvērunt. Numquam cīvēs sē recēpērunt at semper mīlitēs rēgis fūgērunt. Vigilēs dispositī sunt; pontēs frāctī sunt et secūribus caesī sunt. Cīvēs urbis huius pācem amāverant. Tamen quamquam mīlitēs nōn fuerant, cīvēs validī fuerant et mox mīlitēs bonī factī sunt. Diū pugnātum est. Tandem cīvēs vīcērunt et hodiē nōs sumus libērī quod eques fortis hoc dīxit: "Rēx nōbīs nōn benignus est. Līber erō aut moriar."
passages.english:
Before his companions a brave horseman stood and said this: "We are citizens who live in a kingdom. The king who does not live here has authority in this kingdom. We give the king everything he asks and have given for many years. We do all the commands of the king. Meanwhile what does the king give us? What do we receive from the king? Do we receive honor or injury? Which does the king want? Peace? Or war? What ought we to do? What are we able to do? At my house there are weapons. I will take up these weapons. You too take up weapons. Do not restore them to their places. We will fight for the citizens to the end of life!"

From that place those citizens went out and began to fight with great force. Then a serious war was begun. The citizens surrounded the forts and camps of the king and destroyed them with fires. The citizens never retreated but the king's soldiers always fled. Watchmen were posted; bridges were broken and cut down with axes. The citizens of this city had loved peace. Nevertheless although they had not been soldiers, the citizens had been strong and soon became good soldiers. The fighting went on for a long time. At last the citizens conquered and today we are free because the brave horseman said this: "The king is not kind to us. I will be free or I will die."
passages.notes: The American Revolution in Latin! The horseman echoes Patrick Henry (1775). Honōremne an iniūriam = 'Honor or injury?' — -ne introduces the question. Līber erō aut moriar = 'I will be free or I will die.'
vocab_hints: Līber erō aut moriar = I will be free or I will die

passages.title: Cap. XL · Sāturnālia
passages.latin:
Rōmae, temporibus antīquīs, septimus diēs nōn erat diēs quiētis. Semper virī et fēminae labōrābant. Sed saepe fēriae erant. Tum virī nōn labōrābant sed multum edēbant et multum bibēbant et ad lūdōs ībant. Puerī et puellae ad scholam nōn ībant.

Magnae fēriae erant mēnse Decembrī et 'Sāturnālia' appellābantur. Per hās fēriās nūllus labōrābat. Servī nōn labōrābant et togās habēbant et per viās ambulābant. Omnēs erant laetī et per viās "Iō Sāturnālia! Iō Sāturnālia!" exclāmābant. Omnēs amīcīs dōna dabant. Dōna erant cēreī. Multī cēreī in tēctīs clārās flammās dabant. Prīmō hae fēriae per trēs diēs manēbant. Posteā hae fēriae septem diēs manēbant.

Puerī et puellae erant laetī per fēriās. Puerīs et puellīs quoque dōna dabantur. Dōna erant sigilla. Sigillum est parvum. Factum est ex terrā. Parvī puerī et puellae sigilla amant. Diēs cum sigilla dabantur 'Sigillāria' appellābantur.

Per Sāturnālia omnēs aliōs amābant, omnēs erant amīcī. Omnēs cēnās magnās parābant ubi servī edēbant et dominī cēnam dabant.
passages.english:
In Rome, in ancient times, the seventh day was not a day of rest. Men and women always worked. But there were often holidays. Then men did not work but ate much and drank much and went to games. Boys and girls did not go to school.

There were great holidays in the month of December and they were called 'Saturnalia.' During these holidays no one worked. Slaves did not work and had togas and walked through the streets. All were happy and shouted through the streets: "Io Saturnalia! Io Saturnalia!" All gave gifts to friends. The gifts were wax candles. Many candles gave bright flames in houses. At first these holidays lasted three days. Afterward these holidays lasted seven days.

Boys and girls were happy during the holidays. To boys and girls too gifts were given. The gifts were little clay figures. A sigillum is small. It is made from clay. Small boys and girls love the figures. The days when figures were given were called 'Sigillaria.'

During Saturnalia everyone loved others, all were friends. All prepared great dinners where slaves ate and masters provided the dinner.
passages.notes: The Saturnalia (Dec. 17–23) — Rome's great winter festival. Roles reversed: masters served slaves. Gifts of candles (cēreī) and clay figurines (sigilla). Sigillaria was the final day. Connects to Christmas in the next passage.

passages.title: Cap. XL · Chrīstus Nātus Est
passages.latin:
Per Sāturnālia omnēs aliōs amābant, omnēs erant amīcī. Omnēs cēnās magnās parābant ubi servī edēbant et dominī cēnam dabant. Omnēs erant benignī, omnēs erant laetī.

Nunc nōs quoque mēnse Decembrī fēriās habēmus. Diēs paene habēmus quae erant fēriae Rōmānōrum antīquōrum. Multa facimus quae Rōmānī faciēbant. Dōna damus et sumus benignī et laetī. Nōs fēriās habēmus quod Chrīstus nātus est.

Fīnis.
passages.english:
During Saturnalia everyone loved others, all were friends. All prepared great dinners where slaves ate and masters provided the dinner. All were kind, all were joyful.

Now we too have holidays in the month of December. We have days almost like those which were the holidays of the ancient Romans. We do many things which the Romans did. We give gifts and we are kind and joyful. We have holidays because Christ is born.

The End.
passages.notes: The culminating passage of the entire Maxey reader. The author draws a direct line from Saturnalia to Christmas: Nōs fēriās habēmus quod Chrīstus nātus est — 'We have holidays because Christ is born.' nātus est = perfect passive of nāscor. A beautiful conclusion for a Christian Latin curriculum.
vocab_hints: nātus est = is born / was born (perfect passive of nāscor)
Chrīstus nātus est = Christ is born
